The results show that the 656-day periodicity of the signal from pixel 10889 is due to its neighbouring pixel, 10992, which contains the strong source, PKS 2155-304, whose period is about 644 days.

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 2155-304 as a southern TeV blazar for cascade simulations; CTA could detect strong IGMF broadening, while assumed cutoffs and spectral index materially change rejection probabilities.

quotes.quote: For this study we considered two different blazars: PKS2155-304 visible from the southern hemisphere and H1426+428 visible from the northern hemisphere, which combine, in principle, good properties for developing an extended component. The corresponding TeV names for these sources as shown in Figure fluxvsz are TeV-J2158-3013 and TeV-J1428+4240.

Alternative intrinsic source spectra for PKS 2155-304 with energy cut-offs in E _ cut =5 TeV and E _ cut =20 TeV were considered to estimate the impact of high energy photons in the broadening effect. Results show that in the case of a 5 TeV cut-off, rejection probabilities drop below 0.1 for all fields and observation times. This is consistent with the fact that, for this case, fewer high energy photons are being injected in the intergalactic medium, producing a less significant amount of cascade photons. On the other hand, a cut-off in 20 TeV shows no significant changes in relation to the 10 TeV cut-off spectrum, perhaps suggesting that, in spite of the higher cut-off, the amount of injected photons is not enough to produce detectable improvements in the rejection ratios.

For the analysed source PKS 2155-304 corresponding to the southern site, results show that CTA should be able to detect IGMF with intensities stronger than 10 ^ 14.5 G within an observation time of 100 hours.

sources.summary: The paper uses six TANAMI spectral energy distributions of PKS 2155 304 to benchmark a multi-zone blazar jet model, finding strong constraints on acceleration, magnetization, jet power, and emission physics.

quotes.quote: For the first application of our new model we have chosen PKS 2155 304, which is a relatively nearby (redshift z=0.116 ) high-peaked BL Lac. It has been extensively studied by several multi-wavelength campaigns (e.g. Hess12 , Madejski16 , Krauss16 , henceforth K16) that found the source in a variety of spectral states; the wealth of data available makes it an ideal source to benchmark any AGN jet model. We will be focusing on the datasets produced during extensive monitoring by the TANAMI multi-wavelength program, which involved a variety of instruments operating in different bands (K16).

The most notable trend emerging from these individual fits is that the bulk acceleration process always lasts until the magnetization is smaller than 1 and of the order of 2-3 10^ 2 , meaning that the jet always transitions from a Poynting-dominated base ( 1 ) to a kinetic-dominated outer region ( 1 ) as it accelerates. This transition is also consistently required when modelling the SEDs of TeV BL Lacs with one-zone models (e.g. Tavecchio16 ). Despite this departure from equipartition between kinetic and magnetic powers, we always find that the required jet power is sub-Eddington, and comparable to the accretion rate inferred from modelling the optical data.

We find that the jet in PKS 2155 304 has to become strongly particle-dominated at the dissipation region( _ diss 0.02 , including cold protons) in order to match the Fermi /LAT data, which we reproduce purely through SSC.

sources.summary: The paper uses TeV photons from the distant AGN/BL Lac PKS 2155-304 to constrain photon-ALP coupling, fitting its broadband SED with a one-zone SSC model and deriving a 1.2 G source magnetic field.

quotes.quote: Recent studies reveal that an irregular local energy spectrum of PKS 2155-304 has been used to constrain the photon-ALP coupling ( Abramowski2013 ). PKS 2155-304 is a powerful and well-studied TeV gamma-ray source ( Abramowski2010, Aharonian2009a, Aharonian2007, Aharonian2005a ). This BL Lac may offer the possibility for the conversion of photon-ALP in the magnetic field which along the path of propagation ( Falomo1993, Smith1995, Abramowski2013 ). So PKS 2155-304 is a good target for the ALP research at high energies ( Horns2012 ).

In this paper, we obtain some new constraints on ALP properties based on photon-to-ALP conversion probability through TeV photons detected from a distant AGN PKS 2155-304. First, we fit the broadband SED of PKS 2155-304 with a one-zone SSC model. Based on the fitting of its broadband SED, we can obtain the strength of the magnetic field B=1.2 \ G around PKS 2155-304, and the survival probabilities of these Tev photons.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2155-304 as a bright HBL blazar with long-term multiwavelength variability, including H.E.S.S. activity states and a 2016 optical/UV-only flare.

quotes.quote: The blazar PKS 2155-304 (redshift z = 0.116 ) is one of the brightest HBL type object, located in the Southern Hemisphere. Its strongly variable non-thermal emission was observed in different energy bands for already more than 40 years. The object was discovered in the radio frequencies as part of the Parkes survey Shimmins74 , and later identified as a BL Lac type blazar Hewitt80 . Since 2004, in the very high energy ray regime, PKS 2155-304 was frequently observed with the H.E.S.S. telescopes, and this resulted in the discovery of different activity states including low flux level and flaring activity of the object.

In 2016, there was also flaring activity observed in multiwavelength observations of PKS 2155-304, but in this case, the flare was detected only in an optical and ultraviolet regime. No counterpart either in X rays or in rays has been observed. The multiwavelength data provide good coverage of both bumps in the SED of PKS 2155-304 both for 2015 and for 2016.

sources.summary: The paper discusses PKS 2155-304 as a BL Lac object whose H.E.S.S. and Fermi LAT spectra were used to search for photon-ALP spectral irregularities and derive exclusion constraints.

quotes.quote: Results with PKS 2155-304 After the first predictions of 2007PhRvL..99w1102H , one of the first attempts to constraint ALP with gamma rays was made by H.E.S.S., using the data from the BL Lac object PKS 2155-304 2013PhRvD..88j2003A . In this work, a search for irregularities induced by the photon--ALP mixing in the spectrum was performed, and schematically shown in Figure fig:spectral_irregularity_scheme .

Observations of PKS 2155-304 Another study using the Fermi LAT data from the PKS 2155-304 was carried out by 2018PhRvD..97f3009Z . The used data were taken from Fermi LAT observations in the energy range of 100 MeV -500 GeV .

The best fits with and without ALPs were compared to the observed spectrum, and the result is shown in Figure fig:Fermi_PKS_2155-304 . A joint likelihood was calculated; parameter space regions were excluded with 99.9 confidence level and compared with the previous results from H.E.S.S. 2013PhRvD..88j2003A and with the Fermi LAT observations of NGC 1275 2016PhRvL.116p1101A in Figure fig:Fermi_PKS_2155-304 .

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 2155-304 as a BL Lac test case, fitting several flaring SED states and favoring weak external radiation for its orphan optical flare.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155-304 is a well-known blazar in the southern hemisphere and also has bright and variable emissions, particularly in ray energies. It is a relatively nearby high synchrotron-peaked (HSP) BL Lac object at redshift of 0.116. An orphan optical flare lasting a few months was reported for PKS 2155-304 in 2016 2019hepr.confE..27W .

As a BL Lac object, PKS 2155-304 is not expected to have strong BLR and DT radiation. Indeed, no emission line is observed in its spectrum, posing an upper limit of 1.1 10^ 41   erg s^ 1 on its BLR luminosity. Therefore, we consider two cases for the external radiation field in the modeling of PKS 2155-304.
The orphan optical flare (Flare 3) arises when the flaring zone occurs at a distance (i.e., =3.92 pc) far beyond the BLR, the characteristic radius of which is r_ BLR =10^ 3 pc.

sources.summary: The paper finds PKS 2155-304 has a significant 614-day periodic signal compatible with earlier work, plus a linear trend, autoregressive noise, and time-window periods around 618 days.

quotes.quote: The next sources with the most significant periodic signal are PKS 2155-304 and TXS 1902+556, with p_ values below 0.005. For TXS 1902+556, a period of 1120 days ( 3.1 years) is identified. This value is not in agreement with previous literature periodic analysis, , where a period of 3.8 years is found in the low significance level ( >2.5 ). On the other hand, for PKS 2155-304, the fitted periodic signal 614 days ( 1.7 years) is compatible with literature. Both sources show also a linear trend and an auto-regressive noise behaviour.

For PKS 2155-304, the periodic components are also found with p-values below 10^ 2 . Windows 1 and 2 present a period higher than the entire light-curve period of 613 days. Window 3 and 4 present a period below this value. For window 5, the best fitted model corresponds to an AR(1) stochastic component, a linear trend and a sinusoidal with a second harmonic component.

sources.summary: The paper presents PKS 2155-304 as a bright X-ray-selected BL Lac object with blue, featureless spectra, redshift constraints, broadband variability, and possible 4.16- and 7.0-year optical periods.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155-304, the prototype of the X-ray selected BL Lac objects and TeV ray emitter (Chadwick et al. 1999), is one of the brightest and the best studied objects. Its spectrum from 3600 to 6800 appears blue (B-V < 0.1) and featureless (Wade et al. 1979). A 0.17 redshift was claimed from the probably detected weak [ O III ] emission feature ( Charles et al. 1979), which was not detected in Miller McAlister (1983) observation. Later, a redshift of 0.117 was obtained from several discrete absorption features (Bowyer et al. 1984).

PKS 2155-304 varies at all observation frequencies and is one of the most extensively studied objects for both space-based observations in UV and X-ray bands (Treves et al. 1989; Urry et al. 1993; Pian et al. 1996; Giommi et al. 1998) and multiwavelength observations (Pesce et al. 1997 ).
For the two broad minima, the F test is used to check their reality. The significance level is 93.8 for the 4.16-year period and 96.2 for the 7.0-year period.

sources.summary: The paper characterizes PKS2155 304 as a bright ultraviolet HBL, X-ray-variable EGRET source, and first southern-hemisphere blazar detected in TeV gamma rays.

quotes.quote: PKS2155 304: This BL Lac is very bright in the ultraviolet and is classified as a HBL. It is highly variable in X-rays. It is strongly detected by EGRET with a hard spectrum (index 1.71 0.24 ). It was first reported by the University of Durham group working in Narrabri, Australia who saw a signal at the 6.8 level of significance. Upper limits were also reported ; .

It was dramatically confirmed by the report at this symposium by the HESS group who saw it at the 10.1 level in just 2.2 hours of observation. This is the first blazar detected in TeV gamma rays in the southern hemisphere.

sources.summary: The paper uses the z=0.117 blazar PKS 2155-304 to test intergalactic gamma-ray absorption, finding H.E.S.S. TeV data match the predicted steepened spectrum.

quotes.quote: As an example here, we pick the next best observed source, the blazar PKS 2155-304, which is situated at a redshift of 0.117 and for which there are good recent TeV spectral data obtained by the H.E.S.S. air Cerenkov telescope array (Aharonian 2005). This source had earlier been reported by the Durham group to have a flux above 0.3 TeV of 4 10^ 11 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , (Chadwick 1999), close to that predicted by Stecker, de Jager Salamon (1996) using a simple synchrotron self-Compton (SSC) model. Using absorption results obtained by Stecker and de Jager (1998) and assuming an approximate dn/dE E^ 2 photon source spectrum (corresponding to a flat SED), which would apply near the very high energy Compton ``peak'' in the quasi-parabolic log-log SED of a typical SSC model (Stecker, de Jager Salamon 1996), Stecker (1999) predicted that this PKS 2155-304 would have its spectrum steepened by 1 in its spectral index between 0.3 and 3 TeV and would show a pronounced absorption turnover above 6 TeV.

We revisit PKS 2155-304 here, comparing the H.E.S.S. data with a photon source spectrum assumed to be approximated by an E^ 2 which was then steepened by e^ (E_ ) with (E_ ; z = 0.117) as shown in Figure trunctau . The resulting spectrum is shown in Figure PKS2155 along with the H.E.S.S. data. We also found that the best power law fit of our calculated absorbed spectrum had a spectral index of -3.35 which is in full agreement with the H.E.S.S. result on the observed spectrum of -3.32 0.06 (Aharonian 2005). It can therefore be seen that the steep spectrum of PKS 2155-304 observed by the H.E.S.S. group can be explained by intergalactic e^ + e^ absorption.

sources.summary: The paper reports XMM-Newton evidence for possible inverse-Compton X-rays in PKS 2155--304, interpreting its concave spectra as mixed synchrotron and IC emission.

quotes.quote: This paper reports the first discovery of possible inverse Compoton (IC) X-ray emission below 10 keV in the typical high-energy peaked BL Lac object (HBL) PKS 2155--304. Two XMM-Newton observations performed in 2006 reveal that the 0.6--10 keV X-ray spectra of the source harden ( 0.1--0.3) at break energies of 4 keV. The concave X-ray spectra of the source could be easily interpretated by a mixture of a steep component (i.e., the high energy tail of the synchrotron emission) and a flat one (i.e., the low energy side of the IC emission). However, the steep spectra ( 2.3) in the hard X-rays indicate that the synchrotron emission still domiantes over the IC one, while the latter is effectively present and perceived as flattening the synchrotron spectrum in this energy range.

The quasi-simultaneous optical-UV-X-ray spectral energy distributions (SEDs) obtained with \ suggest that the concave X-ray spectra of the source could be the result of down-ward shift of the synchrotron peak frequency to the optical band, incuring the IC emisssion to become more important in the hard ( 4 -10 keV) X-ray band with respective to other cases in which the synchrotron emission peaks in the UV-soft-X-ray range. This discovery provides a new clue for a smooth transition between HBLs and the low-energy peaked BL Lac objects (LBLs).

sources.summary: The paper models the 2006 flare of TeV blazar PKS 2155--304 with synchrotron self-Compton emission, requiring a compact, high-Lorentz-factor, particle-dominated jet region.

quotes.quote: H.E.S.S. observed TeV blazar PKS 2155--304 in a strong flare state in 2006 July. The TeV flux varied on timescale as short as a few minutes, which sets strong constraints on the properties of the emission region. By use of the synchrotron self-Compton model, we found that models with the bulk Lorentz factor 100 , the size of the emission region 10^ 15 cm, and magnetic field 0.1 G explain the observed spectral energy distribution and the flare timescale a few minutes.

This model with a large value of accounts for the emission spectrum not only in the TeV band but also in the X-ray band. The major cooling process of electrons/positrons in the jet is inverse Compton scattering off synchrotron photons. The energy content of the jet is highly dominated by particle kinetic energy over magnetic energy.

sources.summary: The paper models PKS 2155--304's minute-scale TeV flares as emission from compact relativistic blobs moving within a more moderately relativistic blazar jet.

quotes.quote: There are two cases of blazars (Mrk 501 and PKS 2155--304) with flaring TeV emission that varies on timescales of 3-5 minutes (Albert et al. 2007; Aharonian et al. 2007). This variability timescale is much shorter than the light crossing time of the gravitational radius t_ lc hours of the supermassive black holes of these blazars (for inferred masses M_ BH 10^9 ). This implies a very compact ray emitting region.

On the other hand, the jets of PKS 2155--304 and Mrk 501 have been resolved on sub-pc scales and show patterns that move with moderate Lorentz factor _ j 10 (Piner Edwards 2004; Giroletti et al. 2004), much less than the one needed for TeV rays to escape.
In this paper, we propose a jets-in-a-jet model as a plausible source of the TeV flares in Mrk 501 and PKS 2155--304. We postulate the existence of blobs that move relativistically within the jet.

sources.summary: The paper uses simultaneous Fermi and HESS observations of the BL Lac PKS 2155-304 to set upper limits on EBL absorption and disfavor high-density EBL models.

quotes.quote: The blazar PKS 2155-304, a high peak frequency BL Lac object at redshift z=0.116 , was the target of a multiwavelength campaign in late August and early September 2008, which included observations by the Fermi Gamma-Ray Space Telescope and the HESS atmospheric Cherenkov telescope aharonian09 . It is these observations to which we now turn our attention. The ray SED from this campaign can be seen in Fig. \ SED2155 . aharonian09 found that the Fermi SED derived from data between MJD 54704-54715, the period of the TeV observations, can be described by a simple power law with spectral index =1.81 0.14 .

The upper limit on _ is calculated from equation ( taumax ) for these two extrapolations, and the results are shown in Fig. \ tauEBL2155 along with the absorption optical depth predictions of several EBL models. As expected, the steeper GeV index provides stronger constraints to the EBL models.

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 2155-304 as a strong intermediate-redshift VHE source whose quiescent spectrum can reveal low-energy EBL effects but also has an intrinsic break.

quotes.quote: Shown are results for 1ES 1101-232 ( z=0.186 ), a hard spectrum ( _ VHE 3 ) distant source, whose discovery at VHE energies enabled to derive strong limits on the EBL density in the optical to near-infrared , and PKS 2155-304, which is a fairly strong VHE source (about 20 Crab in the quiescente state) with a softer spectrum ( _ VHE 3.3 ) located at an intermediate redshift of z=0.116 . In the case of 1ES 1101-232 the hard measured spectrum in combination with the larger distance leads to a very hard intrinsic spectrum (i.e. harder than anticipated by simple leptonic and hadronic models), even for the low EBL model. Such a hard intrinsic spectrum coupled with the relative weakness of the source ( 2 Crab) leads to a simulated spectrum which does not largely increase the energy range covered compared to the measurement of 1ES 1101-232: neither at low energies (due to the hard spectrum) nor at high energies (the strong EBL attenuation suppresses the signal below the NGCTS sensitivity). Even for the case of the two very different assumed EBL models, no strong difference in the simulated spectra is apparent, which could be used to differentiate between the models. This is different in the case of PKS 2155-304, where, for the different EBL models, very different spectra are expected to be measured at lower energies. The behavior at low energies will be discussed further in the next section.

An example for this procedure is shown in Fig. Fig:LowEnergiesExample for the quiescence spectrum of PKS 2155-304 ( z=0.116 ). An observation time of 20 h has been assumed, which could easily be extended given that this is a steady flux state.

In other cases, e.g. PKS 2155-304, the break between GeV and TeV range is stronger than expected from EBL attenuation, therefore an additional, source intrinsic break is expected somewhere between the two energy bands. Currently, the statistics for this energy range for the sources considered here is not yet sufficient to correctly model the break. Therefore, for the analysis in this paper the lower edge of the low energy fit range is chosen to start above the /LAT energy band (defined as the 5th highest photon in energy reported in , e.g. 40 GeV for PKS 2155-304).

sources.summary: The review describes H.E.S.S. observations of an exceptional PKS 2155-304 flare and uses its light curves to constrain energy-dependent photon propagation.

quotes.quote: Here, the case of the observation of an exceptional flare of the PKS 2155-304 ( z = 0.116 ) by H.E.S.S. is described in more detail. In 2006, the H.E.S.S. experiment detected aharonian07 an exceptional flare of this source, with a high flux (10,000 photons recorded in 1.5 hours) and a high variability (rise and fall times of 200 s during the night of the 28th of July). The over-sampled light curve of the flare is shown in Fig. fig:7 in two different energy bands.

A 95 confidence limit of 100 s/TeV was obtained for the linear correction to the dispersion relations. The MCCF method leads to a limit of the E ^l_ QG > 7.2 10^ 17 GeV and the CWT to a confirmation of this value with a limit of the E ^l_ QG > 5.2 10^ 17 GeV. The work in progress with Likelihood fit of the time-lag with individual photons provides a preliminary lower limit of E ^l_ QG > 2 10^ 18 GeV abramowski10 , thus approaching considerably the limits found with Fermi GRBs.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes PKS 2155-304 as a bright BL Lac object, subtracting host-galaxy light and finding long-term red-noise optical variability with a break timescale.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155-304 is an optical bright X-ray selected BL Lac type object ( m_ V 14^ mag ) at a redshift of z=0.116 1993ApJ...411L..63F . Ground based observations in the near-infrared have been used to separate the relatively faint ( M_ =-24.4^ mag ) elliptical host galaxy from the bright nucleus 1998A&A...336..479K . 1992ApJ...400..115S and 1997A&A...325...27H observed a variation of the optical flux of 2 and 10 respectively per day.

The optical R-band light curve measured with ROTSE is shown in Figure fig_rotse_lightcurve . The contribution of the host galaxy ( 15.1^ mag ) is subtracted here and in the following. The gaps in the light curve are caused by bad weather periods or when PKS 2155-304's position is too close to the sun.

sources.summary: The review describes PKS 2155-304 as a blazar whose spectrum and fast correlated variability test EBL excesses and reject secondary-gamma interpretations.

quotes.quote: The first H.E.S.S. spectrum on PKS 2155-304 provided another possible hint, requiring a sharp upturn and hard rise in case of the highest EBL estimates at 1-5 , but only if the EBL in other wavebands were kept as low as galaxy counts . These anomalous features disappear considering a slightly higher overall EBL level (especially in the UV part), which would cancel the inflection break and excess hardness. Neglecting the EGRET spectrum, taken more than 10 years before and of low significance, the resulting SED could still be typical for a blazar, with an IC peak at few TeV . The PKS 2155-304 data therefore could not rule out an extragalactic origin for the 1-5 \ excess.

At present, only three distant blazars have been measured up to 6-10 TeV: PKS 2155-304 (z=0.116), 1ES 1426+428 (z=0.129) and 1ES 0229+200 (z=0.140). The fast, correlated variability and spectral steepness of PKS 2155-304 above 1 TeV rules out a secondary origin for the gamma-rays in this source.

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 2155-304 as a VHE AGN variability benchmark, citing its H.E.S.S. flare PSD, RMS-flux behavior, amplitude range, and flux distribution.

quotes.quote: In the VHE energy domain the only case in which Fourier analysis of the light curve has been performed is the PKS 2155-304 2006 flare observed by H.E.S.S. The PSD for the 1 minute binned light curve is compatible at 90 confidence level with a power law of index =2 in the frequency range 10^ 4 ,10^ 3 Hz . Another important feature of AGN light curves is a linear relation between the root mean square (RMS) amplitude and the mean flux of groups of contiguous bins of the light curve. This proportionality implies that fluctuations around the mean value, i.e. variability, are enhanced when the flux is higher. This has been observed in X-ray for many AGN and other accreting objects, like binary systems . At VHE such behavior has been detected in the well studied PKS 2155-304 and Mkn 421 .

Fractional RMS amplitudes ranging from 20 to 60 have been observed in the VHE light curve above 200 GeV of PKS 2155-304 . Values of F_ rms for most of the Fermi 2LAC sources are found to vary between 20 and 80 . We simulate AGN-like light curves of the flux above 100 GeV, the typical energy threshold for VHE observatories. To produce time series exhibiting a power-law PSD and a linear RMS-flux relation, we follow the method proposed by Uttley and McHardy . The light curve (t_i) is obtained through an exponential transformation of a linear, aperiodic time series with zero mean, x(t_i) : (t_i)= [ x(t_i) ] The Timmer and K o nig algorithm is used to produce the input time series x(t_i) . This algorithm produces linear time series with a power-law PSD of arbitrary slope. It must be noted that, as a consequence of the exponential transformation, the PSD of the resulting light curve (t_i) is different from that of the input time series x(t_i) . However, for the broad continuous PSD observed in AGN, this distorting effect is relatively small and can be neglected . The exponential transformation of Eq. eqn:exp_lc enhances points above the mean while reducing points below the mean. As a consequence, when the variance of the Timmer and K o nig time series x(t_i) increases, flares in the light curve (t_i) becomes more prominent compared to the dips. The aspect of the resulting light curves (t_i) is thus influenced by the variance of the input time series x(t_i) , a parameter that is inherent to the light curve simulation algorithm but has no clear physical interpretation A possible workaround to this problem would be to assume a log-normal distribution for VHE AGN fluxes. Under this assumption, the statistical properties of the light curves can be related to the mean and variance of the input series x(t_i) , eliminating the need of this additional parameter. However, as of today, log-normal distributions of AGN fluxes have not been observed in VHE, if the entire light curve of the source is considered (see, for example, the bi-modal distribution of PKS 2155-304 flux over the 2005-2007 period as observed by H.E.S.S. ).
The highest frequency will be 1 minute ^ 1 , as in the case of PKS 2155-304 2006 flare light curve measured by H.E.S.S.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 2155--304 as a radio-loud blazar with jet-dominated variable emission and reports optical-polarization QPO evidence with 13 and 30 minute components during gamma-ray activity.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155--304 is a blazar, a subclass of radio-loud active galactic nucleus (AGN) for which the relativistic jet is oriented close to the line-of-sight of the observer Urry2009 . Active galactic nuclei are powered by accretion of matter onto a 10^6-10^9 supermassive black hole (SMBH). The observed emission is dominated by the jet and is characterized by intense and rapid brightness fluctuations across the electromagnetic spectrum, and the presence of polarization at optical to radio wavelengths. exhibits significant variability on timescales ranging from minutes HESS2007 to years Fan2000, Kastendieck2011 . Short timescale variations, from minutes to hours, probe emission regions with sizes comparable to the gravitational radius of the central SMBH, while variations on timescales of months to years probe the jet structure.

Monitoring of the intra-day optical polarization of revealed the first evidence for quasi-periodic oscillations in the polarized flux of an AGN. The periodogram showed the existence of a periodic component at T 13 min, detected at >99.7 significance, and T 30 min, which was found to be nominally significant at >99 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes PKS 2155 304 with H.E.S.S. CT5 monoscopic data, reporting a long exposure, strong detection, thousands of excess events, and a spectrum measured to low energies.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155 304 The data set, filtered as explained in Sect. data_quality , comprises 138 runs. The total live time of this data set is 56.0 hr, 43.7 hr taken in 2013 and 12.3 hr taken in 2014. During these observations, the source zenith angle ranged from 7 to 60 , with a median value of 16 .

The analysis found that the source is detected with a significance of 42 , with 4000 excess events. The corresponding distribution of the excess significance of all skymap bins is shown in the top-right panel of Fig. pks2155_theta2 . The width of the observed excess is approximately compatible with the simulated point spread function (PSF; shown in the inset on Fig. pks2155_theta2 ).

sources.summary: The paper studies PKS 2155--304 as an X-ray-bright high-energy-peaked blazar, finding curved X-ray spectra, hard excesses, variability, and a spine/layer jet explanation.

quotes.quote: The synchrotron peak of the X-ray bright High Energy Peaked Blazar (HBL) PKS 2155 304 occurs in the UV-EUV region and hence its X-ray emission (0.6--10 keV) lies mostly in the falling part of the synchrotron hump. We aim to study the X-ray emission of PKS 2155 304 during different intensity states in 2009 2014 using XMM Newton satellite. We studied the spectral curvature of all of the observations to provide crucial information on the energy distribution of the non-thermal particles. Most of the observations show curvature or deviation from a single power-law and can be well modeled by a log parabola model.

We find that the X-ray excess in PKS 2155--304 is difficult to fit in the one zone model but, could be easily reconciled in the spine/layer jet structure. The hard X-ray excess can be explained by the inverse Comptonization of the synchrotron photons (from the layer) by the spine electrons.

sources.summary: The paper models PKS 2155-304’s optical/UV and X-ray spectra with a broken log-parabola, connecting the spectral curvature to energy-dependent particle escape in the jet.

quotes.quote: Using XMM-Newton observations spanning over 12 years for the BL Lac PKS 2155-304, we show that the optical/UV and X-ray spectra can be well represented by a broken log parabola model. Further, we show that such a spectrum can indicate the energy dependence of the electron escape timescale from the main acceleration zone. This novel approach, besides addressing the observed difference in the photon spectral indices, also tries to explain the spectral turn over in far UV/soft X-rays as a result of the radiative losses.

PKS 2155-304 is a BL Lac class of blazars located at a redshift z=0.116 . Its synchrotron component peaks at UV energies 2016ApJ...831..142M and the broadband spectrum reflects a smooth broken power-law function 1999ApJ...521..552C,2005A&A...442..895A . An extensive study of PKS 2155-304 was carried out by XMM-Newton at optical/UV (180-600 nm) and X-ray (0.15-12 keV) energy bands 2017ApJ...850..209G,2014MNRAS.444.3647B on different epochs spanning more than a decade.

sources.summary: The paper analyses PKS 2155-304 X-ray light curves, finding a higher-flux 2007 epoch whose excess variance deviates and produces inconsistent local non-stationarity signs.

quotes.quote: We analyze X-ray light curves of the blazars Mrk 421, PKS 2155-304, and 3C 273 using observations by the Soft X-ray Telescope on board and archival data. We use light curves of length 30-90 ks each from 3-4 epochs for all three blazars. We apply the autoregressive integrated moving average (ARIMA) model which indicates the variability is consistent with short memory processes for most of the epochs. We show that the power spectral density (PSD) of the X-ray variability of the individual blazars are consistent within uncertainties across the epochs.
The average flux of PKS 2155-304 during the second epoch (obs2-2007) is higher by a factor of 2 compared to the other two epochs.

In the case of PKS 2155-304, we observe that the excess variance in obs1-2004 and obs3-2010 are well within the expected scatter assuming a PSD slope of 2.5 while that for obs2-2007, in which the average flux is larger by a factor 2, are not (Figure Figure:exvar ).

sources.summary: The paper reviews PKS 2155-304 spectral constraints on axion-like particles, derives updated H.E.S.S.-based bounds, and shows magnetic-field assumptions affect whether the pulsar-favored region is partly excluded.

quotes.quote: Ref. tested how much the limits from the Fermi LAT spectrum of PKS 2155-304 are impacted by different choices of magnetic field models. Finally, set constraints from TeV spectra of PKS 2155-304 and PG 1553+113 (Fig. 6 in ), which again partially overlap with Fermi LAT bounds from NCG 1275. In Sec. sec:updatePKS , we derive updated bounds from PKS 2155-304 using H.E.S.S. data.

Updated bounds from PKS 2155-304 with H.E.S.S. The bound derived in relates to two possible scenarios for the magnetic field: Turbulent magnetic field in the inter-galactic medium and turbulent magnetic field in the intra-cluster medium. In Fig. fig:astro_constraints , the resulting limits from are shown for B_ RMS =1 G in the cluster, and B_ RMS =1 n G for the inter-galactic magnetic field. The latter scenario leads to a bound which partially excludes the signal region favored by the PSR data set.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 2155 304 as a bright BL Lac/TeV blazar and analyzes 13 Suzaku observations, finding large-amplitude intraday variability in soft and hard X-rays.

quotes.quote: The blazar PKS 2155 304 ( _ 2000.0 = 21h 58m 52.07s, _ 2000.0 =- 30 ^ 13 ^ ' 32.1 ^ '' ) was listed in an early catalogue of BL Lac objects 1980ApJS...43...57H , has a redshift z = 0.116 0.002 1993ApJ...411L..63F and is the brightest object in the UV and X-ray bands in the southern hemisphere. It was classified as TeV blazar by detection of very high energy (VHE) ray emission by the Durham MK6 telescopes 1999ApJ...513..161C and later confirmed as a VHE ray emitter by H.E.S.S. (High Energy Stereoscopic System) at a 45 detection 2005A&A...442..895A .

We examined the light curves of 13 X-ray pointed observations taken by Suzaku satellite for the TeV emitting HSP blazar PKS 2155 304. These observations were taken over the entire operational period of the Suzaku satellite and the data were available in the public archive. We searched for IDV and its timescales, HRs, time lags between soft and hard X-ray energies, and did PSD analyses of the LCs for the total X-ray energy range to search for the power-laws of the red-noise and any possible QPOs, and we also searched for correlation between the average fluxes and IDV timescales for each individual pointed observation. Our conclusions are as follows: 1. The fractional variability F_ var measurements clearly show that this blazar shows large-amplitude IDV for all 13 pointed observations in both soft and hard X-ray bands of XIS onboard the Suzaku satellite.

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 2155-304 optical polarimetry at z=0.116, finding a negative fitted coefficient and a wavelength dependence attributed to intrinsic astrophysical origin rather than LIV.

quotes.quote: The linear polarization observations of these five blazars are presented in Table tab:observation , including the following information for each object: the energy bands in which polarization is observed; the observed linear polarization angles; and the corresponding uncertainties of the polarization angles. These polarization observations have been obtained simultaneously in five optical bands ( UBVRI ) for all blazars. The redshifts of the five blazars 3C 66A, S5 0716+714, OJ 287, MK 421, and PKS 2155-304 are 0.444, 0.310, 0.306, 0.031, and 0.116, respectively.

As shown in Figure fig:fit , the polarization angles of blazars S5 0716+714 and PKS 2155-304 are proportional to E^2 with a negative coefficient, and the best-fitting values are =(-9.63 4.640) 10^ 8 ( _ 0 =0.320 0.058 rad) and =(-5.32 3.587) 10^ 7 ( _ 0 =1.645 0.018 rad), respectively. While the rest of the blazars show a positive coefficient with the fitting parameters =(3.386 1.249) 10^ 7 ( _ 0 =0.623 0.024 rad), =(6.546 22.769) 10^ 6 ( _ 0 =0.046 0.482 rad), and =(6.226 7.715) 10^ 7 ( _ 0 =1.544 0.094 rad) for 3C 66A, MK 421, and OJ 287, respectively. Our constraints show that except for 3C 66A at the 5.4 confidence level and PKS 2155-304 at the 3.0 confidence level, the data set of the other three blazars are consistent with the possibility of no LIV at all (i.e., = 0 ) within the 1.8 confidence level.

sources.summary: The paper models PKS 2155-304 as an HBL, finding SSC spectra can fit observations for particular R/L and density values and estimating high jet power.

quotes.quote: We modeled five different SEDs of blazars, three for BL Lac object, one each for OJ 287 (z = 0.306), S5 0716+714 (z = 0.31) and PKS 2155-304 (z = 0.116), and the rest two for FSRQ 3C 454.3 (z = 0.859). The BLL OJ 287 has exhibited diverse spectral phases, from LBL to LBL+HBL . For BLLs broadband SEDs, we have taken LBL phase of OJ 287, IBL S5 0716+714 , and HBL PKS 2155-304 .

For HBL PKS 2155-304, we have model spectra with CD < 1. We find that for R/L > 0.01, the model SSC spectra explain the observation well with size L 8.1 10^ 13 cm at n_e = 10 ^ 16 cm ^ 3 . Similarly, to explain the two days ray variability for PKS 2155-304, the electron density should be order of 10 ^ 15 cm ^ 3 .

For BLL the jet power of best modeled SED for OJ 287 (left panel of figure fig:sscOj287a b), S5 0716+714 (middle panel of figure fig:sscOj287a b) and PKS 2155-304 (right panel of figure fig:sscOj287a a) are 3.3 10^ 52 , 6.8 10^ 52 and 1.0 10^ 53 erg/s respectively.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2155-304 as a TeV BL Lac whose NuSTAR spectra vary in flux and curvature without a photon-index/flux correlation.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155-304 PKS 2155-304 is a BL Lac at z = 0.117 Falomo1993 , detected well above the sensitivity limit at TeV energies Chad1999 . Giommi2002babs reported results from three BeppoSAX observations, during which the spectral curvature remained in a narrow range (0.27-0.3) while the flux changed from 2.5 to 8.3 10^ 11 erg cm^ 2 s^ 1 . showed that in this source the X-ray emission is the high-energy end of the synchrotron emission, and that the SED peak location was falling outside the observational X-ray range. observed PKS 2155-304 nine times between 2012 and 2013.

As shown in Fig. gamma1 , this HBL object has a in the range 2.58-3.03 and does not show any evidence for a correlation between the photon index and the flux. On the other hand, the curvature parameter switched between negative and positive values and is correlated with the source flux (P _ cc =0.92 and P(<)=0.0004), as in the case for Mkn 421, see Fig. beta .

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 2155-304 as an extreme BL Lac with minute-scale variability and a hard X-ray concavity similar to Mrk 421, potentially explained by the same cascade model.

quotes.quote: The most extreme examples, with a time variability of some minutes, are PKS 2155-304 Aharo07 and Mrk 501 Alber07 . This extremely short timescale variability may indicate that at least a part of the very high energy (VHE) rays is produced in the close vicinity of their SMBHs.

A similar concave shape in the X-ray range has been observed before by Madej16 in the case of PKS 2155-304. Moreover, other observations of Mrk 421, again in the low state, observed an excess in the hard X-ray energy range Accia21 .

In our opinion, it is a natural explanation of the concave shape of X-ray radiation observed not only in Mrk 421, but also in PKS 2155-304. The future observation of BL Lacs objects in hard X-rays should verify the explanation we discussed here for this curious spectral feature.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2155-304 as the variant HBL: its X-ray peak often lies below the observed band, it overlaps Mrk 421 in curvature behavior but differs in luminosity-peak trends.

quotes.quote: The source PKS 2155-304 is the truly variant member of our set in a number of respects. In fact, the spectral analysis yields a log-parabolic index a>2 , and relatedly, E_p is less than 1 keV. It was difficult to evaluate the SED peak location with , and because it often falls below the observational X-ray range, as shown in Fig. 7.

The source PKS 2155-304 covers a region in the E_p^*-b plane overlapping that of Mrk 421 in Fig. 8. On the other hand, the same figure shows that the source does not appear to follow a similar trend in the E_p^*-L_p^* plane.

sources.summary: The paper reports the first VLBI polarimetry image of PKS 2155 304 and combines data to improve its jet-speed measurement, finding C1 moves at about 0.93c.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155 304 was observed at a single epoch at 15 GHz with dual-circular polarization, and we present the first VLBI polarimetry image of this source. For 1ES 1959+650 and PKS 2155 304, the current observations are combined with the VLBA observations from our earlier paper to yield improved apparent speed measurements for these sources with greatly reduced measurement errors. The new apparent speed measured for component C2 in 1ES 1959+650 is 0.00c 0.04c (stationary), and the new apparent speed measured for component C1 in PKS 2155 304 is 0.93c 0.31c .

Among the most remarkable variability events are the 200 s variability timescale detected for PKS 2155 304 in July 2006 by H.E.S.S. \ (Aharonian et al. \ 2007), and the 3-minute variations detected for Mkn 501 in June 2005 by MAGIC (Wagner 2007a). Such rapid variations suggest extremely small emitting volumes and/or time compression by large relativistic Doppler factors, e.g., 100 for PKS 2155 304 (Aharonian et al. \ 2007).

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 2155-304 as a simultaneous H.E.S.S.-Fermi example: its FGST spectrum matches LBAS values, shows a possible 1 GeV break, and supports the VHE extrapolation strategy.

quotes.quote: The published example of such a simultaneous observation is the joint H.E.S.S.-Fermi campaign on PKS 2155-304 between 25^ th August 2008 and 6^ th September 2008 , . The FGST spectra measured during the campaign is consistent with the values given in the LBAS. However, the spectra in shows a break in the Fermi spectra at 1 GeV. Since it is not known to us if this break is a stable feature in the GeV spectra throughout the three month period used to get the LBAS values, we simply take the single power-law fits from LBAS. Given this we expect the extrapolations to VHE regime to lie slightly above the true VHE measurements, provided the correction due to the EBL absorption was correct (assuming that the EBL used here truly represents an upper limit).

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 2155-304 as a benchmark BL Lac for photon/axion mixing, contrasting its redshift, critical energy, source attenuation, and distance with 3C 279.

quotes.quote: Since we expect the intergalactic mixing to be more important for larger distances, due to the more prominent role of the EBL, we chose two distant astrophysical sources as our benchmark AGNs: the radio quasar 3C 279 (z=0.536) and the BL Lac PKS 2155-304 at z=0.117. We summarize in Table tab:3c279 the parameters we have considered in order to calculate the total photon/axion conversion in both the source and in the intergalactic medium.

The plots show differences in the axion boost factors obtained for 3C 279 and PKS 2155-304 due mostly to the redshift difference. The inferred critical energies for the mixing in the source are E_ crit =4.6 eV for 3C 279 and E_ crit =69 eV for PKS 2155-304, while for the mixing in the intergalactic medium we obtain E_ crit =28.5 GeV (the same for both objects).
In the case of PKS 2155-304, the situation is different from that of 3C 279 due to the very low photon-attenuation at the source and, mostly, due to the smaller source distance.

sources.summary: The paper reviews PKS 2155-304 as a BL Lac flare source, deriving potential Lorentz-violation scales from HESS timing measurements while noting the delay is not significant.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155-304 . HESS Collaboration published an interesting VHE flare of the BL Lacertae object PKS 2155-304 , which locates at z=0.116 , more distant than Markarian 421 and Markarian 501. They made use of the modified cross correlation function and got a time lag 20 s between lightcurves of two different energy bands. However, the delay is not sufficiently significant. They reported that the mean difference of two energy bands in this case is 1.0 TeV, while the mean quadratic difference is 2.0 TeV ^2 , hence we get the potential LV scales to be E_ QG,L 2.6 10^ 18 GeV and E_ QG,Q 9.1 10^ 10 GeV accordingly. They are both about one magnitude larger than our robust values.

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 2155-304 as a BL Lac benchmark for ALP effects, contrasting its redshift, critical energy, and lower source-distance attenuation with 3C 279.

quotes.quote: Since we expect the intergalactic mixing to be more important for larger distances, due to the more prominent role of the EBL, we chose two distant astrophysical sources as our benchmark AGNs: the radio quasar 3C 279 (z=0.536) and the BL Lac PKS 2155-304 at z=0.117. We summarize in Table tab:3c279 the parameters we have considered in order to calculate the total photon/axion conversion in both the source and in the intergalactic medium.

The plots show differences in the axion boost factors obtained for 3C 279 and PKS 2155-304 due mostly to the redshift difference. The inferred critical energies for the mixing in the source are E_ crit =4.6 eV for 3C 279 and E_ crit =69 eV for PKS 2155-304, while for the mixing in the intergalactic medium we obtain E_ crit =28.5 GeV (the same for both objects).
In the case of PKS 2155-304, the situation is different from that of 3C 279 due to the very low photon-attenuation at the source and, mostly, due to the smaller source distance.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2155 304 as the brightest southern BL Lac from UV to TeV bands, with measured redshift, optical/NIR variability, polarization, and X-ray PSDs showing red-noise variability.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155 304 PKS 2155 304 was first identified as a BL Lac by Schwartz et al. \ (1979) and Hewitt Burbidge (1980), and is the brightest BL Lac object from the UV to TeV energies in the southern hemisphere.

A redshift of 0.117 0.002 was given by Bowyer et al. \ (1984) but careful spectroscopy of galaxies in the field of PKS 2155 304, by Falomo et al. \ (1993) demonstrated that the redshift of the source is 0.116 0.002. Simultaneous observations of PKS 2155 304 in the optical and near-infrared bands showed significant variability on a wide range of timescales (e.g., Domini, Abraham Galo 2006; Osterman et al. \ 2007; Dolcini et al. \ 2007). PKS 2155 304 has shown a linear optical polarization of 3 7 with a polarization position angle varying between 70 ^ and 120 ^ (Tommasi et al. \ 2000).

A quantitative analysis of all 14 PSDs of PKS 2155 304 allowed us to find its broad-band X-ray variability to be very well described for most observations by a single power-law component denoting red-noise at lower frequencies with white noise present at high frequencies.

sources.summary: The paper studies low-state optical polarimetry of PKS 2155-304, modeling its emission as a shocked inhomogeneous synchrotron jet and linking polarimetric behavior to quiescent gamma-ray emission.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present multiband optical polarimetric observations of the VHE blazar PKS 2155-304 made simultaneously with a H.E.S.S./Fermi high-energy campaign in 2008, when the source was found to be in a low state. The intense daily coverage of the dataset allowed us to study in detail the temporal evolution of the emission and we found that the particle acceleration timescales are decoupled from the changes in the polarimetric properties of the source. We present a model in which the optical polarimetric emission originates at the polarised mm-wave core and propose an explanation for the lack of correlation between the photometric and polarimetric fluxes. The optical emission is consistent with an inhomogeneous synchrotron source in which the large scale field is locally organised by a shock in which particle acceleration takes place.

Finally, we use these optical polarimetric observations of PKS 2155-304 at a low state to propose an origin for the quiescent gamma-ray flux of the object, in an attempt to provide clues for the source of its recently established persistent TeV emission.

sources.summary: The paper finds PKS 2155-304 has a nearly flat GeV spectrum inconsistent with a maximal cascade model, so direct source gamma rays dominate and cascades only moderately modify the spectrum.

quotes.quote: The observed 0.1-100 GeV band spectra of Mrk 501, PKS 2155-304 and 3C 66A have photon indices close to =2 with almost no curvature of the spectrum over the entire energy range. Such a ``flat'' spectral energy distribution is difficult to obtain in the ``maximal cascade'' model. Although the ``universal'' cascade spectrum has average photon index close to =2 in the 0.1-100 GeV band, its deviation from the pure powerlaw behavior is significant, so that this model is in contradiction with the observed spectrum for these blazars. This suggests that the dominant contribution to the observed source spectrum in the cases of Mrk 501, PKS 2155-304 and 3C 66A comes from direct gamma-rays from the source as shown in Fig. mrk-pks-3C66 . Addition of the cascade contribution to the intrinsic emission component in Mrk 501, PKS 2155-304 and 3C 66A leads only to a moderate modification of the source spectrum in the GeV energy range.

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 2155-304 as a blazar template to simulate CTA spectra and separate intrinsic emission from EBL attenuation at its redshift.

quotes.quote: Here, exemplarily, the case of PKS 2155-304 is studied following the approach described in . tbp  For different levels of the EBL density, generated by scaling the EBL model from , the intrinsic source spectrum is reconstructed and used to simulate the corresponding spectrum as measured by CTA (Fig.  Fig:LowEnergiesExample ). The resulting attenuations for the scaled EBL models are shown in Fig.  Fig:LowEnergiesPKS2155PLResults1 left panel.

In the CTA simulated spectrum two distinct regions in energy can be identified: (i) the unabsorbed region, roughly from 40 to 100 GeV for the redshift of PKS 2155-304 of z=0.116 (red band), where no or little EBL attenuation is present and the intrinsic spectrum can be observed directly, and the (ii) absorbed region at energies >100 GeV, where the EBL attenuation can be measured (blue band). In each of the two bands the spectrum is characterized by a power law dN/dE E^ and the derived photon indices , shown in Fig.  Fig:LowEnergiesPKS2155PLResults1 right panel, are then investigated.

sources.summary: The paper highlights PKS 2155-304 as a bright BL Lac target for CTA studies of stationary states, hadronic flare scenarios, and minute-scale to sub-minute variability.

quotes.quote: Currently, this appears possible only for a few bright sources such as the BL Lac PKS 2155-304. Dealing with real stationary fluxes (if any) will at last allow a good statistical approach to the gamma-ray AGN samples, by comparing objects in the same activity state. This will become possible with CTA.

One recent example in which AGN jets interact with gas envelopes from red giant stars and emit proton synchrotron radiation can even explain the very rapid variability seen during the big flare of PKS 2155-304 in 2006.

In particular, the most probable flare _ d derived for H.E.S.S. is of the order of 5 min, a typical value for the H.E.S.S. light-curve of PKS 2155-304 in 2006. With CTA, this value is expected to shift to 100 s, a factor of 3 shorter than H.E.S.S. The probability of occurrence and detection of a given ultra-fast flare as presented in the beginning of this section can be assessed by comparing the cumulative distribution of the flare durations as derived from our MC simulations (Fig. fig:var_cta_cumdist ). If short events are present in PKS 2155-like light-curves, these will be better sampled by CTA, up to the high resolution limit of the instrument. In the PKS 2155-304 light-curve as seen by H.E.S.S. in 2006 , the shortest variability event was of 173 s, which from simulations has an estimated probability of occurrence and detection by an instrument like H.E.S.S. of 13 (a rare event).

sources.summary: PKS 2155--304 is described as a repeatedly monitored HBL whose 2006 outburst showed minute-scale VHE doubling, X-ray/VHE correlations, and weaker optical/VHE correspondence.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155--304 ( z=0.116 ): This famous HBL has been monitored with H.E.S.S. multiple times since its first detection in 2002. The source was initially found in its quiescence till the exceptional ray outburst occurred in July 2006, with the VHE flux 10 times above the average level, and the VHE flux doubling timescales of the order of a few minutes . The MWL data collected around the time of the 2006 flare were recently presented and extensively discussed in (see also ). These revealed strong X-ray/VHE flux correlation at high flux levels, weakening however at lower flux levels, and characterized in addition by much smaller X-ray flux changes (factor 2 ) than those observed in the VHE range. No exact optical--VHE correlations were found in the 2006 flaring data, although some relation between the long-term flux evolution at low (optical/radio) frequencies and VHE flaring events was noted ( ; see Figure f-2155 ).

sources.summary: The paper targets PKS 2155-304 as a bright TeV blazar/BL Lac at z=0.116, noting H.E.S.S. coverage and its surrounding cluster as useful for ALP spectral-irregularity tests.

quotes.quote: In the following, irregularities are searched for in the energy spectrum of the bright TeV blazar PKS 2155-304 measured by H.E.S.S  . PKS 2155-304 is a powerful TeV emitted BL Lac object located at redshift z = 0.116 . This source is chosen here for two reasons. First, it has been extensively observed by H.E.S.S. and the high available statistics makes possible a precise determination of the spectrum  . Second, a small galaxy cluster of radius 372 kpc is observed around this source  , meaning that a significant magnetic field is expected in the vicinity of the source .

sources.summary: The paper describes extensive H.E.S.S. observations of PKS 2155-304, confirms spectral variability in the 2006 flare, and constrains its 2008 SED peak.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155-304 is extensively observed by H.E.S.S. since the beginning of the experiment which recorded an exceptional flare in 2006 . This event has been divided in 7 flux bins and the data taken in 2008 during a multi-wavelengh campaign makes the last data set for this source.
During the 2006 flare, PKS 2155-304 exhibited spectral variability which is confirmed here.

Three sources have their \ SED peak constrained by the \ and data : Mrk 421, PKS 2005-489 (full data set) and PKS 2155-304 (2008 data set). The peak energy is in the GeV range (Table table:max ). For Mrk 421, this result is obtained during a flaring episode which is not the case of the 2 other sources.

sources.summary: The paper reports earlier 630-day evidence for PKS2155 304, identifies optical and gamma-ray peaks, and notes decreasing significance with correlated-band frequencies.

quotes.quote: We found some evidence Sandrinelli2014a of a quasi-periodic oscillation of T 630 days in PKS2155 304 (z=0.116), corresponding to twice the optical period originally proposed by .

For PKS2155 304, peaks at T _ 620 41 \ days and T _ opt 315 25 days are identified, with T _ 2 T _ opt , confirming previous results in , found by applying different procedures. The associated uncertainties were calculated by adapting the mean noise power level method of to red-noise spectra.

We note that in the case of PKS2155 304 the significances of the optical and ray quasi-periodicities seem to decrease in recent years. The fading of the major periods may be connected to a decreasing trend of the intensity of the source, as observed in the case of PKS0537-441 Sandrinelli2016b . Considering also the results for BL Lac see and Table S Sandrinelli2017 , the main conclusion of our analysis is that the three sources reveal quasi-periodicities with a moderate yet non-negligible statistical significance. The quasi-periodicities occur at the same frequency in both the optical and rays for BL Lac and PG 1553+113, and at correlated frequencies for PKS2155 304.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes PKS 2155--304 during a 2010 optical outburst, linking its flux, polarization rotation, steady variable SED, and inferred jet parameters to a shock-in-jet model.

quotes.quote: An analysis is presented of the optical polarimetric and multicolour photometric ( BVRJ ) behaviour of the blazar PKS 2155--304 during an outburst in 2010. This flare develops over roughly 117 days, with a flux doubling time 11 days that increases from blue to red wavelengths. The polarization angle is initially aligned with the jet axis but rotates by roughly 90^ as the flare grows.

We find that the photopolarimetric behaviour for the high flux state can be attributed to a variable component with a steady power-law spectral energy distribution and high optical polarization degree (13.3 ). These properties are interpreted within the shock-in-jet model, which shows that the observed variability can be explained by a shock that is seen nearly edge-on.

sources.summary: The paper summarizes PKS 2155 304 as a VHE-detected HBL with strong variability, repeated optical and Fermi-LAT QPO reports, and an optical-polarization QPO detection.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155 304 PKS 2155 304 ( =21^ h 58^ m 52 07, =- 30 13 32 12, J2000 epoch), at z=0.116 Falomo93 and a BH mass of 1.3 10^ 7 M_ Lian03 , was detected in VHE rays by the University of Durham Mark 6 ray telescope Chadwick99 . Since then, comprehensive monitoring in VHE energies revealed short and bright flares as well as long- and short-term variability e.g. Aharonian05,Foschini07,Ghisellini08 . Moreover, Zhang14 discovered a QPO with a period of 317 days in 35-years-long optical data, confirmed by Sandrinelli14 who found a period of 315 days, analyzing different data sets. Sandrinelli14 also studied a 6-years-long Fermi LAT LC between 0.1 and 300 GeV, noticing 2 times the optical period, i.e. a periodicity peak at 650--660 days. Sandrinelli16 confirmed the previous result, obtaining 642 days with 3 significance, and revealed two more peaks, at 61 and 52 days, with 2.58 significance.

sources.summary: The paper models PKS 2155-304 as an HBL test case whose LAT/SMARTS variability is best approximated by electron-luminosity changes, though single-parameter models remain incomplete.

quotes.quote: Using as test beds two bright blazars from the SMARTS sample (PKS 2155-304 and 3C 273), we compute 10 year-long OIR, X-ray, and ray model light curves for different varying parameters. We compare the findings of our theoretical investigation with multi-wavelength observations using various measures of variability. While no single-varying parameter simulation can explain all multi-wavelength variability properties, changes in the electron luminosity and external radiation field in PKS 2155-304 and 3C 273, respectively, can account for most of them. Our results motivate future time-dependent studies with coupling between two or more physical parameters to describe the multi-wavelength long-term blazar variability.

To do so, we compare the findings of our theoretical investigation with observations of two bright blazars from the SMARTS sample: the HBL PKS 2155-304 z=0.116 , pkshess , which is known for its very fast flare at TeV energies Aharonian07 and its weak correlation between optical and GeV rays at lower flux states AtomHess , and the well-known FSRQ 3C 273 z=0.158 , Schmidt3c , whose optical/ultraviolet (UV) spectrum shows a prominent excess of emission interpreted as a contribution from a luminous accretion disc Ulrich81, Soldi . This paper is structured as follows. In Section sec:code we describe the one-zone leptonic model and the numerical approach used to compute time-dependent multi-wavelength photon spectra. In Section sec:blavar we describe how we model long-term flux variability in the context of the one-zone leptonic model of blazar emission. In Section sec:results we apply our methodology to the FSRQ 3C 273 and the HBL PKS 2155-304 in order to study the long-term variability in different ray emission scenarios. We then compare our simulation results against observations of both targets in OIR wavelengths and GeV ray energies using various diagnostics.

sources.summary: The paper reports no significant optical/gamma-ray lag for PKS 2155-304 and finds optical QPO peaks near previously reported 610-620 day and 257 day gamma-ray periods.

quotes.quote: In the sources 3C 279, Mrk 421, CTA 102, Mrk 501, PKS 0454-234, PKS 1424-418 and PKS 2155-304 the most significant ZDCFs have the lag within the error bars, or the lead/lag less than the sampling time bin, i. e. 7 days, meaning no significant average lead/lag between the emissions can be claimed.

PKS 2155-304: The LSP of the optical light curves implied the presence of a 643 d QPO in the blazar PKS 2155-304 as shown in the panel e) of Figure fig:6 . This QPO, 97 significant against red-noise in the optical light curve, is closer within the error to 610 d ray periodic flux oscillations reported in and 620 d QPO reported in Sandrinelli2018 . The 317 d timescale reported by Zhang2014RAA in the R-band light curves spanning 35 yrs, however, was not detected in the V-band light curve presented here. But on the other hand, a 96 significant LSP peak around 259 d, which is very close to the 257 d ray QPO reported in Bhatta2020 , was also observed.

sources.summary: The review describes PKS 2155-304 as the H.E.S.S. blazar data set giving the best linear Lorentz-invariance-violation limit, with high signal-to-background TeV observations and modeled emission timing.

quotes.quote: The best limits for the linear deviation are obtained from PKS 2155-304 data from H.E.S.S. \ , a blazar at z=0.116 . The corresponding data sample with threshold 120 GeV has a large signal-to-background ratio. The emission time distribution is fitted by a superposition of five Gaussian spikes (left panel of Fig. figure7.1.3_LIKE ) while the energy spectrum is consistent with a power law in the range 0.25 - 4.0 TeV . From the calibration studies, it is found that the main uncertainty is related to the event selections and to the parameterizations for the emission time distribution. The obtained 95 CL limit is E_ QG,1 >2.1 \ 10^ 18 GeV for the subluminal case with closely comparable value for the superluminal case.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes long-term optical light curves of the blazar PKS 2155-304, finding about 300-day QPOs and discussing frame-dragging, binary black hole, and jet-precession explanations.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155–304 ( z = 0.116 ) Ah09 is one of the best known blazars and one of the brightest objects from the UV to TeV energies in the southern sky Ca92,Fo07 . With the Parkes survey, PKS 2155-304 has been observed in radio band Sh74 . Using HEAo-1, Sc79 completed its first X-ray observations. Due to its strong and variable X-ray emissions, it was classified as a BL Lac object ZG21 . PKS 2155–304 was first identified as a TeV blazar by the detection of VHE gamma rays by the Durham MK 6 telescopes Ch99 , and then was confirmed by the H.E.S.S. Ah05 . PKS 2155-304 has been observed on diverse timescales over a wide range of frequencies from radio to VHE rays, and shown rapid and strong variability Mi83,Fa00 . Generally, blazar variability timescale ( t_ var ) is divided into three classes Gu04,Ag19 : microvariability (intra-night variability or intra-day variability) (IDV; t_ var less than a day) Wa95 , short-term variability (STV; t_ var from days to few months) and long-term variability (LTV; t_ var from months to several years) Pa20 . Through AGN variability, we can obtain information with respect to their nature. It is important for quasar modelling Fa98 . Optical variability of PKS 2155-304 has been studied for many years. PKS 2155-304 has obvious IDV Pa97,To01,Do06 , STV Ca92,Pe97 and LTV Ka11 in optical band, but it also can stay in a completely stable state for one week He97 . In addition, the optical variability is related to X-ray Do04 and NIR band Li18 . The main focus of this paper is long-term variability in optical V-band in PKS 2155-304. The quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs) in optical band have been found in PKS 2155-304. In V-band, with data from 1970 onwards, Fa00 reported QPOs of T 4.2 yr and T 7.0 yr, and also put forward a possibility of a periodicity of less than 4 yr, but they could not confirm this periodicity because of the lack of data. In an available historical archival data-set (data from twenty-five different astronomical groups) in R-band collected for 35 yr (covering 1979 - 2013), evidence of QPOs with a periodicity of 317 days was reported in Zh14 . Ri10 discussed that the long-term QPOs in PKS 2155-304 might indicate a binary black hole (BBH) model, leading to a signal of periodicity when the secondary BH crosses the disk of the primary BH, similar as the QPOs in OJ 287 Va08, Ha13, Br20 . In addition, with the VRIJHK photometry, Sa14 reported QPOs of T 315 days by using data from the Rapid Eye Mounting Telescope during 2005–2012, which is well consistent with the report by Zh14 . What's more, the overlap of time interval between two works makes the periodicity more robust. A few years later, using the extensive HIPPO data taken during 2009 July 25 to 27, Pe16 claimed QPOs of T 13 min and T 30 min, which is the first evidence of QPOs in the polarization of AGN, and discussed that such fast variations in the optical polarization might generate from an emission region, which is comparable in size to radius of gravity of the central engine, in Doppler boosted jet. Sa18 derived data from the Rapid Eye Mounting Telescope photometry, SMARTS, the Tuorla Blazar Monitoring Program, and combined with data from the Steward Observatory Fermi Blazar Observational Program, ROTSEIII, the All Sky Automated Survey robotic telescopes and archival data collected by Ka11 , and found a periodicity of 315 25 days in R-band, which can be caused by relativistic jet instabilities Pa20, Ka21 or chance fluctuation Sm18, Ho18 . Ch19 reported that a 700 day-long periodicity is found in optical band, as well as in high energy (100 MeV < E < 300 GeV) with the combination of SMARTS, RXTE, Swift/XRT, XMM–Newton, Fermi and H.E.S.S. data, which can be explained by a time-dependent synchrotron-self Compton (SSC) Ab10,Ar21,Jo21 model, except for optical band. In this paper, further evidence of optical QPOs will be given in PKS 2155-304 through long-term variability from the Catalina Sky Survey (CSS) Data Release 2 Dr09 and All-Sky Automated Survey for Supernovae (ASAS-SN) Sh14,Ko17 .

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 2155-304 as a nearby X-ray-selected AGN, combining contemporaneous H.E.S.S.-II and Fermi-LAT spectra and finding ALP reconversion slightly favored.

quotes.quote: Combined spectrum of PKS 2155-304 The nearby X-ray selected AGN PKS 2155-304 is the first extra-galactic very high energy gamma-ray source discovered in the southern sky Chadwick:1999ile . It has been closely monitored, both during periods of quiescence as well as during flares HESS:2010tfm . We consider a quasi-simultaneous observation to avoid the combination of data sets averaged over different flux states.
During contemporaneous observations of PKS 2155-304 with H.E.S.S.-II and Fermi LAT in 2013, a spectral break between the HE and VHE data is observed HESS:2016btr .
In the case of PKS 2155-304, the re-conversion of ALPs leads to an enhanced flux at energies exceeding a few TeV which is slightly favored by the data.

sources.summary: The paper models PKS 2155-304 as a high-synchrotron-peaked BL Lac blazar whose giant 2006 TeV flares and low-state emission can be explained by stochastic multi-blob dissipation.

quotes.quote: The blazar PKS 2155-304 is a high synchrotron-peaked BL Lac object located at redshift z=0.116 . On 2006 July 28, an extremely remarkable outburst of VHE ray emission from this blazar was reported by the H.E.S.S. experiment, with an average flux more than 10 times the low-state level. The variability timescale of this extraordinary flare was as short as approximately 200 s.

Here we demonstrate that the stochastic dissipation model, which is a multi-blob scenario for blazars, can self-consistently explain the giant TeV flares of PKS 2155-304 and the low-state emission before and after the flares, in terms of both multi-wavelength spectral and variability characteristics. The required Doppler factor in this model can be as low as 20, which is a reasonable and typical value for blazar jets.

sources.summary: The paper reports IXPE and Swift-XRT observations of PKS 2155--304, finding the highest measured X-ray polarization among blazars and simultaneous spectral variability.

quotes.quote: For PKS 2155–-304, only one observation was performed by IXPE, at which point the X-ray flux of source is almost the historically low state. Interestingly, the detected highest X-ray polarization among blazars is measured in PKS 2155-–304. We also find that the obvious variability along with the spectral variation is presented in the simultaneously monitoring Swift--XRT data of PKS 2155--304.

1ES 1959+650 z=0.047 ; 1996ApJS..104..251P and PKS 2155--304 z=0.116 ; 1993ApJ...411L..63F are two TeV HBLs 1999ApJ...513..161C,2003ApJ...583L...9H and are also the targets of the IXPE observations. In this paper, we report the first X-ray polarimetric observations of the two HBLs.

sources.summary: The paper presents ISO infrared observations of the BL Lac object PKS 2155--304, finding a power-law synchrotron spectrum, little infrared variability, and negligible host-galaxy contribution.

quotes.quote: The BL Lacertae object PKS 2155--304 was observed by the Infrared Space Observatory in May and June 1996, during a multiwavelength campaign. These are the first observations in the mid-- and far--infrared bands since IRAS. In the observing period, the source showed no detectable time variability at 4.0, 14.3, 60 and 90 m. The spectrum from 2.8 to 100 m is well fitted by a single power law with energy spectral index = 0.4 , intermediate between the flatter radio spectrum and the steeper simultaneous optical spectrum. The overall infrared to X--ray spectral energy distribution can be well explained by optically thin synchrotron emission, with negligible contributions from thermal sources.

sources.summary: The paper describes 2155-304 as the brightest XBL in the UV, with correlated X-ray/UV/optical variability, polarization, and possible infrared color correlations.

quotes.quote: 2155-304, z=0.117 It is the brightest XBL in the UV. The X-ray, UV, and optical light curves are well-correlated, suggesting a common origin of the optical through X-ray emissions, and the X-rays lead the UV by 2-3 hours (Edelson et al. 1995). It has been extensively studied in the UV and X-rays (Pesce et al. 1996; Pian et al. 1996, and reference therein). Polarizations of P_ opt =14.2 ( Pesce et al. 1996) and P_ IR =9.4 0.7 (Mead et al. 1990) and an optical variation of m_ V = 1.85 ( Fan Lin 1999b) are reported. There is an indication for (J-K) to be correlated with (J-H) and (H-K) and H as well (see Fig. 13): (J-K) = 1.04 - 0.04H with = -0.37 and p = 1.9 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2155-304 as a prototype high-energy peaked BL Lac with unusual Compton dominance, high gamma-ray luminosity, and exceptional 2006 VHE activity monitored by HESS.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155-304 have shown for the first time in an HBL a large Compton dominance, high ray luminosities and a cubic relation between X-ray and VHE fluxes.

At opposite sides in the blazar sequence , the well-known, bright blazars PKS 2155-304 and 3C 279 are prototypes of the two blazar classes of high-energy peaked BL Lacs (HBL) and Flat spectrum radio quasars (FSRQ). PKS 2155-304 is a classic HBL, characterized by a spectral energy distribution (SED) peaking in the UV-X-ray and GeV-TeV bands, by the X-ray band dominated by synchrotron emission of high-energy electrons, by the absence (or very low intensity) of the Broad Line Region (BLR) emission (typical HBL upper limits at 10^ 41 erg/s), and by a Compton dominance L_ C /L_ S 1 .
In July-August 2006, PKS 2155-304 entered a phase of exceptional activity at VHE monitored by HESS and other observatories (for a full overview, see ).

sources.summary: The paper discusses PKS 2155-304 as an HBL with strong VHE and X-ray activity, correlated high-state emission, low-state optical behavior, and two-hour variability.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155-304. A HBL object, its strong VHE emission was first detected in 1997 November, at the same time the strongest X-ray emission ever observed and GeV gamma rays were also detected by BeppoSAX and EGRET (Chadwick et al. 1999). A simultaneous observation with HESS, Chandra and the Bronberg optical observatory was carried in the night of July 26-30 2006 when the source was in a high-activity state at gamma-ray band and the gamma ray flux reached 11 times the Crab flux (Aharonian et al. 2009a). The emission between X-ray and VHE gamma-ray is strongly correlated. The broadband SED of this campaign is shown as blue squares in Fig. Fig:1 .

sources.summary: The paper finds that PKS 2155-304, like PKS 1424+240, is better fit by broken-power-law or BLR-absorption models than a simple power law, with a large fit improvement.

quotes.quote: BPL and full BLR absorption scenarios seem to fit well the combined spectra of the blazars PKS 1424+240 and PKS 2155-304. A BPL (full BLR absorption) function is preferred over the PL for the blazars PKS 1424+240 and PKS 2155-304 with a significance of 5 ( 4.8 ) and 12 ( 8.5 ), respectively (see Figure fig:gevTevFits ). The ^ 2 /dof values of PL, BPL and APL fits are 32/9, 3.5/7, 5.3/7 for PKS 1424+240 and 148/8, 5.8/6, and 71/6 for PKS 2155-304, respectively. BPL fits yield =1.4 (PKS 1424+240) and =0.7 (PKS 2155-304), both larger than what electron cooling would predict, which might indicate that an additional mechanism is at work.

sources.summary: The review uses PKS 2155-304 as an active-galaxy SED example, noting its two-component synchrotron/Inverse-Compton structure, variability, and need for simultaneous multiwavelength observations.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:sed shows the SEDs of two very different objects, the Crab nebula and the active galactic nucleus PKS 2155-304 . For these two objects, a two-component structure is observed, the first bump being due to synchrotron radiation and the second one being generally interpreted as due to Inverse Compton effect. In the case of PKS 2155-304 which is a variable source, the determination of the SED requires simultaneous observations at different wavelengths in the framework of a coordinated campaign. Variability correlations may then be found between different parts of the spectrum, thus providing important clues in modeling the object.

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 2155-304 as a bright HBL blazar and studies its two-peaked multiwavelength emission, variability, and X-ray spectral complexity with NuSTAR and XMM-Newton.

quotes.quote: PKS 2155-304 is one of the most extensively studied BL Lac objects. It is a strong emitter of electromagnetic radiation in all observable bands, from radio to very high energy (VHE) rays. Its E F(E) broad-band spectrum reveals two prominent peaks located respectively in the far UV/soft X-ray band, and in the multi-GeV part of the high energy ray band. As such, PKS 2155-304 belongs to the class of jet-dominated active galaxies with the jet pointing close to our line of sight - known as blazars - and, specifically, to a sub-class known as high-energy peaked BL Lac objects, or HBLs (see, e.g., Pad95 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS2155 304 as a very bright blazar with high- and low-state H.E.S.S. spectra that are crucial for identifying the EBL absorption pattern.

quotes.quote: PKS2155 304 ( z = 0.116, 1993ApJ...411L..63F ) is a very bright blazar extensively studied by As in HESS2013, the data of the exceptional July 2006 high state are used (labels 1 to 7), together with observations of the 2008 low state (label 8). These very-high-significance data sets yield excellent quality spectra that are crucial for an unambiguous identification of the EBL-absorption pattern.
Figure EBLRes_SubFig_2 shows this effect for the subset PKS 2155 304 (5), to be compared with Fig. SubFig_3 .
For instance, subsets of PKS 2155 304 show optimal sensitivity in the 1.1 -- 4.94 m band, with precisely fitted EBL levels.

sources.summary: The paper selects PKS 2155--304 for detailed RXTE/PCA blazar analysis, extracts four flares, finds peak energies below 3 keV, and reports harder-when-brighter X-ray spectra.

quotes.quote: As such, among 32 TeV blazars, we singled out 5 objects (i.e., Mrk 421, Mrk 501, PKS 2155--304, PKS 2005--489, and 1ES 1959+650; hereafter ``the five sources''; see Table table:allta ) and for which RXTE /PCA data allow us to obtain high-quality spectra (detailed analysis of the other 27 TeV blazars will be presented in a future study). Furthermore, the flares were selected with the following criteria: 1) individual flares lasted for several days and were covered by at least two observations in both the rise and decay periods; and 2) the minimum total count rate (summed over available PCUs) in 3--25 keV is above 30 /s. In addition, adjacent outbursts following or followed by those flares were also included. Finally, we picked out 20.5 flares for Mrk 421 (an outburst with observations only in the rise or decay period was considered as 0.5 flare), 7 flares for Mrk 501, 4 flares for PKS 2155--304, and only one flare for both PKS 2005--489 and 1ES 1959+650 from the 16-year data (see Figures fig:spectrum and fig:pfevo for the typical flares of each source; also see the observations annotated with `` '' in Table table:allta .

Combining the 3--25 keV spectral shape information and the synchrotron peak energy obtained by fitting all PCA spectra with the log-parabolic model detailed in Section sec:peakes , we found that: for Mrk 421, the peak energy of all the spectra is below 6 keV, which is consistent with the results in 2004A&A...413..489M , 2004ApJ...601..759T and 2007A&A...466..521T (but 2009A&A...501..879T shows that its peak energy could be up to 30 keV); for Mrk 501, the peak energy of most spectra is above 3 keV, and 2008A&A...478..395M shows that its peak energy could be up to 100 keV; for PKS 2005--489 and PKS 2155--304, the peak energy of spectra is below 3 keV; for 1ES 1959+650, the peak energy of most spectra is below 30 keV.

The X-ray spectra, characterized by , display a harder-when-brighter trend during a number of flares of the five brightest sources (i.e., Mrk 421, Mrk 501, PKS 2155--304, PKS 2005--489, and 1ES 1959+650), which is consistent with previous studies.
