If it is a BHB star, the nearest distances to LMC and Sgr dSph are 35.5 0.8 and 24.4 0.8 kpc, and the flight times are 73 6 and 17 7 Myr. The tidal radii of LMC and Sgr dSph are 22.3 5.2 and 3 kpc 2014ApJ...781..121V, 2001MNRAS.323..529H , respectively, thus LAMOST-HVS4 is unlikely originated from the core regions of LMC and Sgr dSph, but the dwarf galaxy origin cannot be exclude completely.

sources.summary: The paper fits Sagittarius dSph RR Lyrae distances but cannot fit its on-sky position because it lies at the PS1 footprint edge; its distance is slightly above but consistent with prior work.

quotes.quote: We fitted the five dwarf galaxies Sagittarius dSph, Sextans dSph, Draco dSph, Ursa Minor Dwarf dE, Ursa Major I dSph. As the Sagittarius dSph lies at the edge of the PS1 3 footprint, we cannot successfully fit its on-sky position (l,b) , but we can fit its heliocentric distance D . This can be clearly seen from Fig. fig:Sagittarius_dSph_fits . For this reason, we don't give any values dependent on the fitted (l,b) position in the tables.
For Sagittarius dSph, our distance estimate of 28.18 kpc is slightly larger than, but within the uncertainties of the 26.3 1.8 kpc result obtained by Monaco2004 .

sources.summary: The paper uses SkyMapper and Gaia to identify metal-poor members of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy, confirming a very metal-poor stellar population and probing its early chemical history.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: the center of the Sagittarius dSph ( _ J2000 = 18h55m19.5s, _ J2000 =-30 d32m43s)

quotes.quote: We find that these two datasets allow for efficient identification of metal-poor members of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy to follow-up with further spectroscopic study. Two of the stars we present have < -2.0 , which adds to the few other such stars currently identified in the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy that are likely not associated with the globular cluster M54, which resides in the nucleus of the system. Our results confirm that there exists a very metal-poor stellar population in the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy.

By adding to the population of the most metal-poor stars known in the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy, we aim to probe its early chemical and assembly history. Here we report the discovery of four photometrically-selected metal-poor stars in the Sagittarius dSph.

sources.summary: The white paper presents Sgr dSph as a nearby dwarf spheroidal whose core hosts M54, and argues that asteroseismology could recover its star formation history, age gradients, and Milky Way infall timing.

quotes.quote: Among the many dwarf spheroidal galaxies, the Sgr dSph is both one of the closest, and more interesting ones. It contains a nuclear star cluster at its core, M54, which is expected to evolve similarly to Cen Carretta2010 . Its distance (25 kpc away) represents the main difficulty for asteroseismic observations; however, our simulations (Sec. sec:seismic_perf ) indicate that solar-like oscilations are detectable at the level of the RC and above, provided a mirror with diameter 90 cm and at least 6 months of observations. These observations could represent the first asteroseismic measurements for an external galaxy. They would allow a determination of the Sgr dSph star formation history, which then may be used to infer possible age gradients and to date when Sgr dSph entered within the sphere of influence of our Galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper compares Sag dSph with the LMC as a non-equilibrium satellite, noting its orthogonal orbit, two stellar streams, associated dark-matter debris speeds, and possible impact on direct-detection annual modulation.

quotes.quote: The most famous non-equilibrium structure is the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy (Sag dSph). This galaxy is moving in an orbital plane orthogonal to the MW's disk, perpendicular to that of the LMC. It has two massive stellar streams wrapped completely around the MW . The associated stellar and DM debris stream is expected to exhibit speeds of 400-500 km/s with respect to the Earth (see also Ref. , who find stream debris to contribute at 350 km/s). These authors find that the SHM is insufficient to describe the non-Maxwellian velocity distribution caused by Sag dSph and that the presence of Sag dSph may reduce the amplitude of the annual modulation of the event rate by as much as factor of two.

sources.summary: The paper models Sagittarius with a double-burst star-formation history, increasing predicted LISA-detectable double white dwarfs relative to the fiducial satellite model.

quotes.quote: We forecast that out of the known satellites, Sagittarius, Fornax, Sculptor, and the Magellanic Clouds can be detected with LISA.

We list stellar masses and distances of known satellites from mcc12 and report the number of LISA detections for our fiducial model in Table table:known_satellites . Of all known satellites, only Sagittarius, Fornax, Sculptor, and the Magellanic Clouds host detectable LISA sources.

The SFH of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy could be modelled by two events: a first one forming 50 of the total stellar mass >12 Gyr ago, and the other forming the remaining 50 < 4 Gyr ago wei14 . When adopting this more appropriate star formation scenario, the number of detections in Sagittarius increases to 10 DWDs for a 4 yr mission lifetime compared to 3 reported in Table table:known_satellites .

sources.summary: The paper links Sagittarius dwarf galaxy pericentre passages to three Milky Way star-formation enhancements and describes Sgr as a satellite repeatedly perturbing the Galactic disc.

quotes.quote: This complex behaviour is ascribed to repeated perturbations induced by the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy (Sgr ) along its orbit , pointing to this satellite as the main dynamical architect of the Milky Way disc. Here, we model Gaia DR2 observed colour-magnitude diagrams to obtain the first detailed star formation history of the 2-kpc bubble around the Sun. It reveals three conspicuous and narrow episodes of enhanced star formation that we can precisely date as having occurred 5.7, 1.9 and 1 Gyr ago. Interestingly, the timing of these episodes coincides with proposed Sgr pericentre passages according to i) orbit simulations , ii) phase space features in the Galactic disc , and iii) Sgr stellar content . These findings most likely suggest that Sgr has also been an important actor in the build-up of the Milky Way disc stellar mass, with its perturbations repeatedly triggering major episodes of star formation.

sources.summary: The paper uses Sagittarius dwarf-galaxy red giants as a comparison sample, noting cobalt deficiency relative to the Milky Way and linking it to weaker SNe II enrichment.

quotes.quote: In order to further investigate the disagreement on the level of Co deficiency or over-enhancement, it is interesting to note the deficiency in Co relative to Fe in the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy. In Fig. sagitplot we compare the present results for Co in LTE and NLTE, compared with Co abundances in 158 red giants of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy by Hasselquist et al. (2017). These authors used the H-band from APOGEE data and found that Co is deficient with respect to stars in the Milky Way.

Therefore, the deficiency of Co in Sagittarius relative to the present paper is not as drastic as in previous results discussed in the literature. A possible explanation of the deficiency in Co in Sagittarius, previously already suggested by McWilliam et al. (2013), is that Sagittarius was less enriched by SNe II relative to the Milky Way, which could be caused by a top-light initial mass function (IMF).

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Sagittarius debris, finding metallicity-dependent stream kinematics and interpreting a diffuse metal-poor component as material stripped earlier from the Sagittarius progenitor’s stellar halo.

quotes.quote: The tidal disruption of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy has generated a spectacular stream of stars wrapping around the entire Galaxy. We use data from Gaia and the H3 Stellar Spectroscopic Survey to identify high-quality Sagittarius members based on their angular momenta. The H3 Survey is largely unbiased in metallicity, and so our sample of Sagittarius members is similarly unbiased.

Comparison to simulations suggests that the diffuse component was stripped from the Sagittarius progenitor at earlier epochs, and therefore resided at larger radius on average, compared to the colder metal-rich component. We speculate that this kinematically diffuse, low metallicity, population is the stellar halo of the Sagittarius progenitor system.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as a disrupted satellite contributing globular clusters, with M54 as a nucleus, distinctive abundances, and an inferred dark halo mass.

quotes.quote: The exception is three initially massive clusters from the disrupted Sagittarius galaxy; one of them (M 54) was presumably the nucleus of this galaxy. Central clusters of galaxies have a special formation history; they lose almost no stars due to dissipation, and can also increase their mass due to the field stars of the parent galaxy falling on them until the disruption of the latter.
All clusters of the Sagittarius galaxy in the entire metallicity range turned out to be below the lower envelope.

Modeling the tidal tail kinematics of the stars of the Sagittarius galaxy in showed that, in order to reproduce the velocity dispersion in the stream from this galaxy, the mass of its dark halo should be M=6 10^ M_ . The masses of other disintegrated galaxies that formed the groups of clusters studied here (Sequoia, High Energy, and Helmi streams 99) are only slightly less than the masses of those listed (see and references therein).

sources.summary: The review links the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy to Gaia's Milky Way phase spiral, star-formation-history peaks, disk-mass buildup, and timing of recent disk passage.

quotes.quote: A massive intruder would in fact excite waves in the disk e.g., Laporte2018 and pointed to the previous pericentric passage of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as the event that caused the observed phase spiral.

Further observational evidence that the Sagittarius dwarf is related to the phase spiral was uncovered by through a stellar population synthesis analysis of the 2 observational HR diagram of stars within 2 kpc from the sun. The star formation history they inferred shows peaks at 5.7 , 1.9 , and 1 Gyr ago, coinciding with the past pericentre passages of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy. This suggests that the Sagittarius dwarf may have played an important role in the build up of the stellar mass of the Milky Way's disk, where it is a curious coincidence that the somewhat extended peak in the star formation history around 5.7 Gyr ago covers the interval in time some 4.6 Gyr ago during which the Sun and the solar system formed. elaborate the model of to further understand how the phase spiral is excited and how it evolves after an impulsive disturbance. They time the emergence of the phase spiral at about 380 Myr after the last passage of the Sagittarius dwarf through the Galactic disk.

sources.summary: The paper discusses the Sagittarius dwarf as a disrupting satellite whose tidal stream wraps the Milky Way, with angular-momentum analysis identifying stream members and older leading and trailing tails.

quotes.quote: Since its serendipitous discovery by ibata94 , the Sagittarius (Sgr) dwarf galaxy has become a poster child for the tidal disruption of satellite galaxies and the hierarchical build up of galactic stellar haloes. With early observations largely restricted to pencil-beam fields of view ivezic00,vivas01,newberg02,martinez-delgado02 , the striking extent of the tidal tails was not revealed until all-sky 2MASS survey became available majewski03 . It was soon realized that dynamical modelling of the tidal tails provides powerful constraints on the gravitational field of the Milky Way (MW).

Comparison against the angular momentum of the Sagittarius dwarf, L _ sgr = _ sgr v _ sgr =(17.9, 2.6, -6.6) (239.5, -29.6, 213.5) = (+360, -5402, -1152) vasiliev20 , indicates that the first Gaussian component, with 32 of our sample, corresponds to tidal debris from the Sgr dwarf galaxy. Notice, however, that the median angular momentum of the stream is slightly different from that of the remnant core.

sources.summary: Sgr is used as an extended-star-formation dwarf comparison whose high Eu/Mg plateau constrains delayed neutron-star-merger enrichment models.

quotes.quote: In addition to the [ Eu/Mg ] data we have measured for Kraken and GSE, we also compare our models qualitatively against [ Eu/Mg ] measured for dwarf galaxies of comparable mass (LMC, Fornax, and Sgr). For Sgr, we rely on Bonifacio00,McWilliam13 who measured these abundances for five stars spanning [ Fe/H ] of -0.1 and -0.5. Star-formation durations for these galaxies (all 10 Gyrs) are sourced from Weisz14sfh - in particular, we adopt their t_ 90 which is the time taken to form 90 of the stellar mass.

Requiring a plateau value of [ Eu/Mg ] 0.5 for 10 Gyr star-formation durations observed in galaxies of comparable mass -- Sagittarius, Fornax, and the Large Magellanic Cloud -- provides an upper bound of t_ delay 1000 Myr (see navy blue curve in bottom-left panel of Figure fig:models ). Galaxies like the LMC, Fornax, and Sgr have much higher [ Eu/Mg ] at fixed metallicity compared to the MW.

sources.summary: The paper cites the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal as an accreted Milky Way satellite and compares its core, tidal streams, and nuclear cluster M54 abundance patterns to Terzan 5 scenarios.

quotes.quote: Evidence for accretion in the MW has been seen in the past, including the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal (Sgr dSph) identified by Ibata_1994 and the more recently discovered Gaia-Sausage-Enceladus GSE; Belokurov_2018,Haywood_2018,Helmi_2018,Mackereth_2019 .

Take, for instance, the case of the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal (Sgr dSph) and its nuclear cluster M54. Recently, Hayes_2020 used APOGEE DR16 to determine median values for [ Mg/Fe ] 0.03 and [ Si/Fe ] 0.12 for the Sgr dSph core, and slightly larger values for both the leading and trailing arms (by no more than 0.15/0.06 dex in the case of [ Si/Fe ] / [ Mg/Fe ] ). These numbers should be confronted with those obtained by Trincado_2021 , who determined for M54 the mean abundances [ Mg/Fe ] +0.2 and [ Si/Fe ] +0.2 . So, both the core and tidal streams of the Sgr dSph have lower [ /Fe ] than its nuclear cluster, thus going in the opposite direction of what is required to explain the chemical discrepancies between Terzan 5 and the bulge field.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Sgr as a comparatively low-mass but close-pericentre perturber of GSE debris, noting its current mass, recent pericentre, dissolution, and larger past mass.

quotes.quote: The two key exceptions to this are the uniquely massive Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy (Sgr).

While a few orders of magnitude less massive at present, Sgr's smaller pericentre makes it an arguably more impactful perturber of the GSE debris. Originally discovered by ibata1995sgr , Sgr has also just passed its most recent pericentre of around 16 kpc, and with its present-day mass of 5 10^8 M_ is expected to dissolve over the next few billions years vasiliev2020last . However, its mass at earlier times was surely much higher, around 10^9 - 10^ 10 M _ niederste-ostholt2010re,bennett2022exploring , or even up to 6 10^ 10 M _ jiang2000sgr,gibbons2017sgr . The unique behaviour of the LMC and Sgr means they may be able to have their orbits or total masses constrained by the discovered local phase space chevrons.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as the strongest known perturber proposed for the Gaia snail, but notes mass, morphology, and encounter-age difficulties with that single-encounter explanation.

quotes.quote: The most common hypothesis is that the strongest disturbance resulted from a passage of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy close to the solar neighbourhood.

The strongest perturbations from a known source -- by an order of magnitude or more -- are due to the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy banik22 . There are some difficulties with a model in which an encounter with Sagittarius is the main stimulus for the snail. (i) In an extensive set of N body simulations, find that they are unable to match the observed amplitude of the snail unless the mass of the Sagittarius dwarf is much larger than the best current estimates, and that whatever the mass they are unable to find a detailed match to the shape of the snail. (ii) The estimated time of the encounter, derived by fitting the shape of the snail, varies by a factor of two or more, depending mainly on the area of the Galactic disc that is included in the sample Estimates of the age of the encounter include 0.3-- 0.9 antoja2018 , 0.5-- 0.8 laporte19 , 0.54 lw21 , 0.3-- 1.3 widmark22 , and 0.2-- 0.6 fra22 .

sources.summary: The paper discusses Sagittarius as a disrupting Milky Way companion whose mass, stream bifurcation, and possible binary-dwarf merger history motivate simulations of its tidal debris.

quotes.quote: The Sagittarius (Sgr) dwarf spheroidal galaxy is an ancient disrupting companion of the Milky Way (MW) whose vast sinuous tidal tails wrap around our Galaxy ibata1995sgr,Majewski2003 . While it is estimated to have a present total mass around 4 10^8 M _ , work shows that Sgr will dissolve within the next Gyr vasiliev2020last , and was likely some orders of magnitude more massive in its past e.g. niederste-ostholt2010re, bennett2022exploring . The ongoing Sgr merger is the archetypal galactic cannibalism event, and stands as a testament to the ever-evolving nature of our home Galaxy, yet many aspects of the Sgr have not yet been explained -- for example, the bifurcation in its tidal stream belokurov2006field,Koposov2012 .

Little residual rotation is detected in the remnant's body today, leaving the hypothesis of the bifurcation resulting from the Sgr hosting a disc in the past disfavoured e.g. see penarrubia2011no . Recent analysis of the Sgr debris with the Gaia data has revealed that the bifurcation in both leading and trailing tails can be traced all the way back to the remnant vasiliev2021tango,Ramos2022 , reaffirming that the stream bifurcation is associated with properties of the progenitor.

sources.summary: The paper links Milky Way outer-disk asymmetries to the last Sgr dSph impact, using simulations to show the dwarf’s passage can produce similar radial-angle substructures.

quotes.quote: In this work, we show new evidence that the ``Monoceros area" likely originates from a disturbance caused by the last impact of the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy (Sgr dSph). The paper is organized as follows. The sample and the analyzed quantities are introduced in Section sec:sample .

In Section sec:testparticle , we use a Milky Way-like test particle simulation with a Sgr dSph impact to the disk and find that the Sgr dSph impact can produce the asymmetric features in _R orbital phase space that are similar to observations. In Section comparison , we compare the asymmetric kinematic features of the test particle simulations to the observed Milky Way disk substructures. We provide a discussion in Section discussion and summarize in Section summary .

We show from a test particle simulation that the Sgr dSph impact could cause clumps in _R space similar to those that are observed. We then use the simulations to associate prominent clumps in _R space with their dynamical origin. From the test particle simulation analyzed in this work, we show that the Monoceros ring-like low V_ substructure and the corresponding clump in the conjugate angle space of radial action can be produced by interaction between the disk and the dwarf galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper compares Sagittarius remnant and stream chemistry, finding distinct Mg tracks that may indicate two chemically different dwarf-galaxy populations.

quotes.quote: Motivated by these simulations, we use APOGEE DR17 chemical data from approximately 1100 stars in both the Sagittarius remnant and stream to look for evidence of contamination from a second dwarf galaxy. We separate the Sagittarius data into its remnant and stream and compare the [ Mg/Fe ] content of the two populations. Comparing the stream and remnant populations, we find regions have distinct [ Mg/Fe ] distributions for fixed [ Fe/H ] , in addition to distinct chemical tracks in [ Mg/Fe ] - [ Fe/H ] abundance space. Furthermore, we show that the two tracks can be fit with unique star formation histories using simple, one zone galactic chemical evolution models. While more work must be done to discern whether the hypothesis presented here is true, our work hints at the possibility that Sagittarius may consist of two chemically distinct dwarf galaxy populations.

sources.summary: The paper treats Sagittarius as a nearby dark-matter-dominated dwarf spheroidal, modeling its halo and J factor for gamma-ray emission from possible dark matter annihilation.

quotes.quote: Of the population of the Milky Way (MW) dSphs, one of the most unique is the Sagittarius (Sgr) dSph. Kinematic analyses indicates that Sgr is a DM dominated galaxy. However, unlike almost all other dSphs, Sgr has an associated population of globular clusters, which are known gamma-ray emitters due to their millisecond pulsar populations . In particular the globular cluster at the center of Sgr, M54, is a well-motivated source for gamma-ray emission. Indeed, a gamma-ray source has been identified to be coincident with the spatial location of M54 .

sources.summary: The paper maps Sagittarius dwarf galaxy RR Lyrae at low Galactic latitudes, fits its core profile, and argues its density break and slow outer decline indicate extended structure.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of 1 500 RR Lyrae of Bailey type ab located in the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy (Sgr). These variables have been detected on two ESO Schmidt fields centred on (l,b)=(3.1 ,-7.1 ) and (6.6 ,-10.8 ), covering an area of 50 deg ^ 2 . We present a surface density map of Sgr based on the spatial distribution of these RRab, allowing us to trace its structure in a region that was still almost unexplored between b=-14 and b=-4 .

The core of Sgr is best fitted by an exponential with a scale length of 4.1 along the major axis. A cross section of this density map revealed a break in the slope occurring at 6 from the highest density region of Sgr and an almost flat density past the break.

sources.summary: The paper models Sagittarius dwarf galaxy microlensing sources, finding longer timescale events, field-dependent event fractions, and high-latitude bias in optical-depth maps.

quotes.quote: We estimate the optical depth, time-scale distribution and fraction of microlensing events originating from sources in the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy (Sgr) lensed by deflectors in the Milky Way. These events have a time-scale longer by a factor 1.3 than the MW/MW events and occur mainly on sources fainter than V 21 mag below Sgr's turn off. The fraction of events involving a source in Sgr depends on the location and extinction of the field and on the limiting magnitude of the survey. The contribution of the MW/Sgr events is negligible ( 1 ) at very low latitudes ( |b| 2 ) but increases continuously towards higher |b| and becomes dominant near the highest density region of the dwarf galaxy. Sgr is present within the fields of current microlensing surveys and any optical depth map inferred from observations will become biased by the presence of Sgr towards higher |b| where the contribution of MW/Sgr events is significant.

sources.summary: The paper describes the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as a Milky Way satellite whose tidal streams may contribute local stars and dark matter relevant to WIMP direct-detection experiments.

quotes.quote: The Sloan Digital Sky Survey and the Two Micron All Sky Survey have traced the tidal stream of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy (Sgr), a satellite galaxy that is located inside the Milky Way Galaxy. Two streams of matter are being tidally pulled away from the main body of the Sgr galaxy and extend outward from it. The leading tail is showering matter down upon the solar neighborhood .

Estimates of the current mass-to-light ratio of the Sgr dwarf (and hence plausibly the tidal tails) are M/L (25-100) . We estimate a range for the local stream dark matter density : _ str = [ 2, 190 ] 10^4 M_ / kpc ^3 = [ 0.001, 0.07 ] GeV/cm^3 , which corresponds to (0.3-25) of the local density of the isothermal Galactic halo, assuming _h = 0.3 GeV/cm ^3 Based on hierarchical clustering, it is likely that a clump contributes 1-5 to the local dark matter density .

sources.summary: The paper measures iron and alpha-element abundances for RGB-tip stars in Sagittarius, describing its dominant population, tidal disruption by the Milky Way, and chemical clues to dwarf-galaxy evolution.

quotes.quote: We present preliminary iron abundances and element (Ca, Mg) abundance ratios for a sample of 22 Red Giant Branch (RGB) Stars in the Sagittarius galaxy (Sgr), selected near the RGB-Tip. The sample is representative of the Sgr dominant population. The mean iron abundance is [ Fe/H ] =-0.49.

The Sagittarius dwarf Spheroidal galaxy (Sgr dSph) is currently disrupting under the strain of the Milky Way (MW) tidal field. The study of the Sgr chemical composition allows us to study at the same time the star formation history of a dwarf galaxy and the relevance of the hierarchical merging process for the formation of large galaxies such as the MW.

sources.summary: The paper cites Sagittarius as a disrupting Milky Way satellite whose tidal tails constrain the halo shape, motivating oblate-halo simulations and Gaia searches for satellite debris.

quotes.quote: This trend culminated with the discovery of the Sagittarius Dwarf galaxy by Ibata94 and the discovery of the Canis Major dwarf Newberg2002,Martin04 . These satellite galaxies are in the process of being torn apart by the tidal field of our Galaxy.

The shape of our galaxy's halo is disputed and in this respect the disruption of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy provides a useful test case. By studying tidal tails from this dwarf galaxy Ibata2001 ruled out the possibility that the halo is significantly oblate (i.e. with an axis ratio of q_ _h < 0.7 ). However, MartinezDelgado2004 suggest that the potential of the Milky Way dark halo, as constrained by observations of the Sagittarius dwarf, is likely to be oblate with q_ _h =0.85 , while modelling of radial velocities of M-giants in the Sagittarius dwarf by Helmi2004 suggests that the halo is prolate with an average density axis ratio within the orbit of Sagittarius close to 5/3 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Sagittarius as a small dwarf galaxy being shredded by the Milky Way, producing tidal streams whose dark matter could affect WIMP detector signals.

quotes.quote: Numerical simulations indicate that galaxies formed by mergers of smaller objects. Recently, one such stream has been found: the Sagittarius stream. On the other side of the Galactic Center is a small dwarf galaxy, Sagittarius, which is being shredded up by our Milky Way: this is a merger in progress. Two tidal streams of stars are seen coming off of the Sagittarius Galaxy. One of them is seen to pass near the Solar System. These streams are likely to contain dark matter, in an abundance that adds an amount (1-20) of the local halo density.

sources.summary: The paper treats Sagittarius as the massive dSph exception whose stars show Type Ia supernova signatures, including Mn/Fe behavior, unlike lower-mass dwarf spheroidals.

quotes.quote: This analysis reveals a clear contribution from SNe Ia in the LMC stars as well as stars belonging to the Sagittarius (Sgr) galaxy, but not for stars in other smaller mass dSph galaxies.

For the Sgr galaxy, the data for globular cluster Palomar 12 ( [ Fe/H ] = 0.8 ) which is suggested to originate from this galaxy is denoted (Cohen ). The detailed [ Mn/Fe ] feature for the Sgr galaxy is shown in McWilliam Smecker-Hane ( ), revealing that the Mn/Fe ratios have an SNe Ia signature in the metal-rich regime, which is consistent with the analysis of elements in 2.

The [ /H ] vs. [ Fe/H ] diagrams where represents Mg or average of elements for stars in the dSph galaxies (i.e., Draco, Ursa Minor, Sextan, Sculptor, Carina, Fornax, and Leo I) present no compelling evidence for the contribution of SNe Ia to stellar abundances in the low- /Fe stars, in contrast with the Sagittarius galaxy and the LMC. It might suggest that massive dwarf galaxies with a mass of several 10^8-10^9 retain the ejecta of SNe Ia in the gravitational potential well and the interstellar matter in these galaxies is enriched by SNe Ia, while this is not the case for lower mass dSph galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper describes Sgr dSph as a 26 kpc dwarf with broad metallicity spread and analyzes two confirmed carbon-star members, including bright IGI95-C3.

quotes.quote: The Sgr dSph galaxy was discovered ten years ago by Ibata et al. ( ). It lies at about 26 kpc from the Sun (Monaco et al. ) and is characterized by a large spread in metallicity, 0.8 [ Fe/H ] 0.0 (see e.g. Bonifacio et al. 2004b), and recent observations reveal the existence of stars with metallicity as low as [ Fe/H ] 3 (Bonifacio et al. 2004a). The adopted interstellar extinction is A_ B =0.66^ mag (NASA/IPAC database, Schlegel et al. ). The two selected stars were confirmed to be carbon-rich and members of the Sgr dSph galaxy by Ibata et al. (1995). According to Whitelock et al. (1996), IGI95-C3, is one of the brightest star in Sgr dSph, with extremely red colors suggesting a large mass-loss rate and/or a low effective temperature (see below the problems encountered during its chemical analysis).

sources.summary: The paper derives an 8.0 Gyr mean age for the dominant Sagittarius dSph population and connects that population to M giants tracing its tidal stream.

quotes.quote: We present a statistically decontaminated Color Magnitude Diagram of a 1 1 field in the core of the Sagittarius dSph galaxy. Coupling this CMD with the most recent metallicity distributions obtained from high resolution spectroscopy we derive robust constraints on the mean age of the stellar population that dominates the galaxy (Pop A). Using three different sets of theoretical isochrones in the metallicity range 0.4 M/H 0.7 and taking into consideration distance moduli in the range 16.90 (m-M)_0 17.20 we find that the mean age of Pop A is larger than 5 Gyr, and the best-fit value is age = 8.0 1.5 Gyr.

Since Pop A provides the vast majority of the M giants that traces the tidal stream of Sgr dSph all over the sky, our estimate resolves the so called ``M giant conundrum'' (Majewski et al. 2003). The time needed by the M giants that currently populates the stream to diffuse within the main body of Sgr and to reach the extremes of the tidal tails once torn apart from the parent galaxy ( 3-4 Gyr) can be easily accommodated into the time lapsed since their birth ( 5.5-9.5 Gyr).

sources.summary: The paper describes the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy as a disrupting satellite whose surviving nucleus and full-sky tidal tails constrain the Milky Way halo, and models its orbit, mass, and stream bifurcation.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The present position of the Sgr dSph is ( , ) = (283.7^ , -30.5^ ), while its heliocentric distance is 25 2 kpc and radial velocity is 137 \ Ib97 .

quotes.quote: The disrupting Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy (Sgr) was discovered by Ibata, Gilmore Irwin in 1994. It was soon realized that the Sgr provided a powerful tool for the study of the Galaxy Ib97 . The nucleus of the Sgr has survived for many orbits around the Galaxy, whilst its tidal tails have now been detected over a full 360^ on the Sky see e.g. To98,Ma03,Be06 . The disrupted fragments of the Sgr diffuse in the Galactic potential. As the pericenter of the Sgr's orbit is 16 kpc, whilst its apocenter is 60 kpc, the debris provides a strong constraint on the Galaxy's halo.

sources.summary: The paper finds the Sagittarius dwarf's stream field has far more BHB stars than its core, implying an old metal-poor population was preferentially stripped from a radially graded progenitor.

quotes.quote: We present a quantitative comparison between the Horizontal Branch morphology in the core of the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy (Sgr) and in a wide field sampling a portion of its tidal stream (Sgr Stream), located tens of kpc away from the center of the parent galaxy. We find that the Blue Horizontal Branch (BHB) stars in that part of the Stream are five times more abundant than in the Sgr core, relative to Red Clump stars. The difference in the ratio of BHB to RC stars between the two fields is significant at the 4.8 level.

This indicates that the old and metal-poor population of Sgr was preferentially stripped from the galaxy in past peri-Galactic passages with respect to the intermediate-age metal rich population that presently dominates the bound core of Sgr, probably due to a strong radial gradient that was settled within the galaxy before its disruption. The technique adopted in the present study allows to trace population gradients along the whole extension of the Stream.

sources.summary: The paper uses MACHO RR Lyrae stars in the Sgr dwarf galaxy to derive an accurate distance and discuss its dynamical evolution and role in bulge microlensing.

quotes.quote: These stars provide important clues for understanding the formation and evolution of the inner Galaxy, the Large Magellanic Cloud and the Sgr dwarf galaxy. A large number of second overtone pulsators (RRe) are found in the LMC and bulge. Finally, the RR Lyrae belonging to the Sgr dwarf yield an accurate distance to this galaxy. Their presence also alerts us of the very interesting possibility of distant sources for bulge microlensing events.

The MACHO project discovered large numbers of RR Lyr in the MW, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), and the Sgr dwarf galaxy (Sgr). Here we will discuss a few astronomical applications of our on-going study of this sample, starting with a brief description of the selection of RR Lyr among various other periodic variable stars (Section 2). RR Lyr are excellent tools to study pulsational physics, and therefore to determine fundamental stellar parameters. They can be used to determine masses, the distance to the LMC, and the ages of the oldest stellar populations, and to compare the old stellar populations of the LMC and the MW bulge (Section 3). RR Lyr are primary distance indicators. They can be used to study the structure of the inner MW (Section 4), and to determine the distance and dynamical evolution of Sgr, thereby aiding in the interpretation of the microlensing optical depth (Section 5).

sources.summary: The paper notes H.E.S.S. observed the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy without detecting gamma rays, and says tidal interaction with the Milky Way prevents a confident central-mass estimate.

quotes.quote: The H.E.S.S. array (consisting of four 107 m ^2 telescopes with a 5 ^ field of view and an energy threshold of 160 GeV ) has obtained an 11 hour exposure of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy. No ray signal was detected, resulting in a flux limit of 3.6 10^ 12 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 (95 confidence) at E>250 GeV, and a corresponding limit on the cross section of 10^ 23 cm ^3 s ^ 1 for an NFW profile and 2 10^ 25 cm ^3 s ^ 1 for a cored profile (for a = 100 GeV 1 TeV neutralino) . Note that the Sagittarius dwarf is undergoing a strong tidal interaction with the Milky Way galaxy , and no confident determination of M_ 0.3 has been possible.
The Sagittarius dSph, for example, is thought to be in the process of complete disruption from tidal interactions with the Milky Way.

sources.summary: The paper treats Sagittarius as a dwarf-galaxy comparison, noting associated metal-poor clusters, contrasting enrichment in metal-rich and metal-poor populations, and suggesting M54 may have migrated to its center.

quotes.quote: Two metal-poor globular clusters in this sample are associated with the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy (Arp 1 and M54, ibata95,law10 ). While the metal-rich stars in Sagittarius clearly have been enriched by the \ (e.g., chou10 ), the metal-poor stars and globular clusters appear to lack \ material. Pb, which has not been examined in any Sagittarius debris, would provide the strongest confirmation of this scenario. While M54 likely formed elsewhere in Sagittarius and later migrated to its center bellazzini08,carretta10 , M22 may itself be the nucleated core (i.e., the central remnant after the outer layers have been stripped away) of a dwarf spheroidal galaxy (dSph) like Centauri ( Cen; dacosta09 ).

sources.summary: The paper excludes Sagittarius from equilibrium dwarf constraints and discusses its disruption, possible pre-interaction mass, and limited impact on the dark-subhalo conclusions.

quotes.quote: We exclude Sagittarius, which is far from dynamical equilibrium, for the present.
Sagittarius is in the process of being completely disrupted by the disk, but estimates of its pre-interaction properties give it a total stellar mass similar to the SMC niederste-ostholt2010 .

In the absence of the MW disk -- e.g., in dissipationless simulations such as those used here -- it is very likely that Sagittarius would be much more massive at z=0 , and our Magellanic Cloud exclusion criteria might also be appropriate for Sagittarius. Regardless, the inclusion or exclusion of one object does not alter the conclusions reached below.

sources.summary: The paper uses HST proper motions of stars near the Sgr dSph tidal radius to infer the dwarf's motion, noting bound material extends to about 8 degrees.

quotes.quote: FIELD 2 is located at ( _ ,B) = (-3.53^ ,4.65^ ) . This places the Sgr stars in this field at 5.8^ from the Sgr dSph. In N body simulations, bound material is found out to 8^ from the Sgr dSph e.g., law05 . Therefore, the Sgr stars identified in FIELD 2 give information primarily about the PM of the Sgr dSph itself, and less so about the kinematics of unbound stream material. Several measurements of the PM of the Sgr dSph already exist in the literature, based on different kinds of data: din05 used the ground-based Southern Proper Motion Catalog 3; pry10 analyzed \ data of three fields; and mas13 analyzed \ data of a foreground globular cluster (NGC 6681), qualitatively similar to the case for our FIELD 2 .

sources.summary: The paper uses Sagittarius dSph galaxy as a chemical comparison, noting its combined metallicity distribution with M54, metal-rich extent, and abundance similarities to Omega Centauri's metal-rich stars.

quotes.quote: compared detailed chemical abundances for Centauri RGB stars and the stars in the Sagittarius dSph galaxy and its nuclear GC, M54. Based on metallicities, the authors concluded that Centauri's stars resemble the metallicity distribution found for M54 and Sagittarius dSph together. In particular, the metal-rich population of Centauri is consistent with the extent of the metallicity distribution of Sagittarius itself. Moreover, the element abundance for the surrounding nucleus of Sagittarius is similar to what is found for the metal-rich stars in Centauri while M54 shows [ /Fe ] abundances in agreement with the metal-poor and metal-intermediate populations of Centauri.

sources.summary: The paper treats Sagittarius as the largest, most metal-rich Milky Way dSph comparison, with extended star formation and metal-rich Zn measurements unlike older components.

quotes.quote: Measurements of Zn from four Milky Way dSph satellites are shown in Fig. fig:azzn . Out of these, the Carina dSph is the smallest and Sagittarius is the largest and most metal-rich; see Table tab:dwarfgalaxies . The individual stars with [ Zn/H ] measurements in Draco and in the main body of Sagittarius do not have age determinations. However, the stellar population in Draco is predominantly old (e.g., Aparicio2001 ). The star formation history of Sagittarius is more extended, and the available Zn measurements are in metal-rich stars, which are expected to be of young and/or intermediate age ( Bellazzini2006,Siegel2007,deBoer2015 ).
For Sagittarius, [ Zn/H ] measurements are not available in the older and more metal-poor component.

sources.summary: The paper describes the Sgr dwarf galaxy as a tidally disrupting satellite whose stripped stellar stream dominates Galactic halo substructure and whose stream geometry can constrain disruption simulations.

quotes.quote: The Sgr dwarf galaxy was first discovered by , who found evidence of a dwarf galaxy within 16 kpc of the Galactic center, on the far side of the Milky Way, that was thought to be in the process of tidally disrupting. The tidal stream of stars stripped from this dwarf galaxy have since been found to dominate the substructure of the Galactic halo e.g. newberg2002,Majewski2003,Belokurov2006,Hernitschek2017 . Though the Sgr dwarf galaxy and the stream of stars that have been tidally stripped from its gravitational grasp have been studied extensively see for a recent review LawMajewski2016 ; we are only starting to understand the dynamical history of this present-day merger.

Refining the spatial distribution of the Sgr dwarf tidal stream using the algorithm described in this paper will help constrain N-body simulations of the Sgr dwarf tidal disruption, and lead to a better understanding of the shape of the Milky Way's dark matter halo.

sources.summary: The paper cites Sagittarius dwarf galaxy/Sgr as an external perturber of the Galactic disk, using prior simulations and studies to connect its passages with vertical phase-space spirals and proposed perturbation times.

quotes.quote: The vertical perturbations are usually thought to be excited by minor mergers, such as the passage of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy through the Galactic disc gmo+13 .

laporte2018 used a set of numerical N-body simulations, in which a Sgittarius-like dSph (Sgr) hits a Milky Way-like host, to follow the orbit of Sgr from the first pericentric passage to the present-day, and illustrate the evolution of the phase-space spirals in the last Gyr.

monari2018 claimed that the disk experienced a vertical perturbation by the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy 1.5 Gyr ago, and related it to the formation of the Coma Berenices moving group. A18 also inferred that the disk perturbation is caused by the passage of Sagittarius dwarf galaxy, but it occurred between 300 and 900 Myr ago.

sources.summary: The paper says Sagittarius has supersolar [Eu/Mg] values and likely developed this enhancement late, since its lowest-metallicity [Eu/Ca] is consistent with solar.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, the trends of [ Eu/Mg ] with [ Fe/H ] are more difficult to understand in the larger dSphs, Fornax and Sagittarius. Both galaxies have supersolar values of [ Eu/Mg ] that are not straightforward to explain, highlighting that our understanding of the process is by no means complete. At the lowest metallicities in Sagittarius, [ Fe/H ] <-1 , the observed [ Eu/Ca ] =-0.04 Hansen18 is consistent with the solar value, therefore it is likely that this enhancement of [ Eu/Mg ] occurred late in the history of the galaxy. However, we note that the abundance ratios of [ Eu/Mg ] in Fornax (which has more extended measurements) show no clear signs of increasing trends of [ Eu/Mg ] with [ Fe/H ] .

sources.summary: The paper treats Sgr dSph as a disrupting dwarf satellite whose tidal stream and globular clusters are traced with Gaia DR2, RR Lyrae, kinematics, and abundance patterns.

quotes.quote: The ongoing disruption of the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy Sgr dSph, sgr0 provides a formidable case study of the ingestion of a dwarf satellite, a process that is generally considered as a main driver for the formation of large galaxies see, e.g. and references therein FBH . Sgr dSph is populating the Milky Way halo with the stars (and, presumably, the dark matter particles) that are lost along two huge tidal tails (Sgr stream) that have been traced with various techniques over a huge range of distances 10-100 kpc, see, e.g. and references therein iba01a,heidi02,m03,fos,heidi07,NO10,matte,prec . The Sgr dSph hosts four globular clusters (GCs) in its main body that, before the discovery of the satellite, were believed to belong to the GC system of the Milky Way (M54, Arp 2, Ter 7, and Ter 8). By analogy, additional Sgr GCs may have been lost in the disruption process and may lie immersed in the Sgr stream.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as a major satellite perturber and notes that associated cluster orbits require detailed Sagittarius evolution modeling.

quotes.quote: Gomez_2015 studied the importance of considering the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) in simulations of the tidal tails of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy (Sgr) around the Milky Way. They found that when the LMC was included, tidal debris from Sgr was found to be misaligned with the present-day orbital plane of Sgr.

We also consider the effects of just the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy and just the Magellanic Clouds on cluster orbits, because these are two of the more massive systems and could help rule out the importance of considering less massive dwarfs when modelling GC evolution.

It is perhaps not surprising that the orbits of clusters associated with Sagittarius are affected by the dwarf itself, with a detailed model for the time-evolution of the Sagittarius dwarf being necessary before it can be determined whether the kinematic properties of these clusters are consistent with once being members of Sagittarius or not.

sources.summary: The paper models the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as a recent perturber of the solar neighbourhood, varying its mass, profile, position, and orbit, and concludes Sgr cannot explain Gaia's vertical disc oscillations.

quotes.quote: We apply our method to the range of possible perturbations to the solar neighbourhood stemming from the recent passage of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy (Sgr), varying its mass, mass profile, and present-day position within their observational uncertainties, and its orbit within different realistic models for the Milky Way's gravitational potential. We find that we are unable to reproduce the observed asymmetry in the vertical number counts and its concomitant breathing mode in velocity space for any plausible combination of Sgr and Milky-Way properties. Both the amplitude and the period of the number-count asymmetry and of the oscillations in the mean vertical velocity produced by the passage of Sgr is in large disagreement with the observations from Gaia DR2. We conclude that Sgr cannot have caused the observed oscillations in the vertical disc or the Gaia phase-space spiral.

sources.summary: The paper studies the Sagittarius dwarf system through M 54 and APOGEE spectra, finding chemical anomalies and extra-tidal evidence consistent with globular-cluster debris in the satellite.

quotes.quote: We present evidence for globular cluster stellar debris in a dwarf galaxy system (Sagittarius: Sgr) based on an analysis of high-resolution H band spectra from the Apache Point Observatory Galactic Evolution Experiment (APOGEE) survey. We add [ N/Fe ] , [ Ti/Fe ] , and [ Ni/Fe ] abundance ratios to the existing sample of potential members of M 54; this is the first time that [ N/Fe ] abundances are derived for a large number of stars in M 54. Our study reveals the existence of a significant population of nitrogen- (with a large spread, 1 dex) and aluminum-enriched stars with moderate Mg depletion in the core of the M 54 + Sagittarius system, which shares the light element anomalies characteristic of second-generation globular cluster stars (GCs), thus tracing the typical phenomenon of multiple stellar populations seen in other Galactic GCs at similar metallicity, confirming earlier results based on the Na-O anti-correlation.

The Sagittarius (Sgr) dwarf spheroidal (dSph) galaxy is one of the closest massive satellites of the Milky Way (MW) Ibata1994 , and has yielded a wealth of observational evidence of ongoing accretion by the MW in the form of persistent stellar debris and tidal streams discovered by Mateo1996 , and extensively studied with photometric and spectroscopic observations over a huge range of distances ( 10--100 kpc) see, e.g., Ibata2001, deBoer2015 using different stellar tracers--including Carbon stars Totten1998 , the first all-sky map of the tail

sources.summary: The paper treats Sagittarius as a disrupting dwarf whose diffuse, low-metallicity stellar halo may have been found by H3 and can test models of dwarf-galaxy stellar halo formation.

quotes.quote: On slightly more massive scales, use the H3 survey conroy19 to find a kinematically diffuse, low metallicity population related to the disrupting Sagittarius (Sgr) dwarf galaxy. These authors suggest that this could be the stellar halo of the Sgr dwarf progenitor.

Indeed, the stellar halo of the (disrupting) Sagittarius has potentially already been uncovered by the H3 survey but also see penarrubia21 johnson20 . Interestingly, identify the diffuse halo component by selecting Sagittarius stars in angular momentum space, in excellent agreement with the outputs of our toy models.

sources.summary: The paper uses Gaia-based proper motions for Sagittarius dSph and places it as the satellite member of the Sagittarius merger group.

quotes.quote: While inspecting this catalog, we found that it lacks the proper motion measurements of two other satellites of the Milky Way, namely Bootes III Grillmair_BootesIII_Styx and the Sagittarius dSph Ibata1994 .

For Sagittarius, we use the catalog that provides \ DR2 based proper motions for this dwarf. From this, we compute the median and uncertainty for the Sagittarius dSph as ( ^ * _ , _ ) = (-2.67 0.45,-1.40 0.40) .

We find that 8 objects belong to this group: 6 globular clusters (namely, Pal 12, Whiting 1, Terzan 7, Terzan 8, Arp 2, NGC 6715/M 54), 1 stream (namely, Elqui) and 1 satellite (namely, the Sagittarius dSph itself).

sources.summary: The paper models the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy around M54 as a triaxial system being tidally shredded, with distinct stellar populations and density profiles beyond the cluster.

quotes.quote: The M54/NGC6715 cluster is at the center of the Sagittarius (Sgr) dwarf galaxy Ibata94,Bellazzini08 . The Sagittarius dwarf galaxy is modeled as a prolate triaxial dwarf galaxy with its axis of rotation tilted approximately 40-60 \ from the sky plane delPino21 with gradients of both density and velocity dispersion so shallow that radial averaging is acceptable. The blue stars in the central 50 pc are kinematically distinct from stars of all colors at larger radii, which belong to the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy and have ages ranging from the last 0.5 Gyr to a Hubble age AlfaroCuello19 .
The Sagittarius Dwarf galaxy is being shredded in the Milky Way s gravitational field Ibata94 to produce a tidal stream wrapped around the galaxy at least twice Ibata20:SgrStream .

sources.summary: The paper describes the Sagittarius dwarf as the first known Milky Way accretion object and reviews modeled tidal disruption and associated globular-cluster membership.

quotes.quote: Sagittarius dwarf (Sgr) Shown as dark blue pentagons in Figs. fig:fehago - fig:space , the Sgr dwarf galaxy is the first known MW accretion object ibata1994 . Law_2010a developed numerical models of Sgr's tidal disruption, and they found that the initial Sgr system may have included 5-9 GCs. A total of 23 GCs may have been associated with the Sgr, where five (Arp 2, NGC 6715 (M54), NGC 5634, Terzan 8 and Whiting 1) have high probability, four (Berkeley 29, NGC 5053, Pal 12, and Terzan 7) have medium probability and two (Pal 12, and Terzan 7) have low probability Law_2010a . Many subsequent studies massari2017, Sohn2018, bellazzini2020 have improved the classification of these GCs based on new observations such as Gaia DR2 and Hubble Space Telescope (HST).

sources.summary: The paper identifies the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as a likely perturber of Milky Way phase spirals, noting its repeated disk crossings and prior simulations of Sgr-Milky Way encounters.

quotes.quote: A promising candidate for the origin of the spirals is the passage of a dwarf galaxy or dark matter subhalo through the Galactic disc with the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy (Sgr) considered a prime suspect laporte2019, bennett2021, bennett2022 . Sgr is the nearest known neighbor to the Milky Way ibata1994 and has an orbit that has likely taken it through the mid plane of the Galaxy several times over the last few Gyr johnston1995 . purcell2011 argued that Sgr was crucial in shaping the Milky Way's bar and spiral arms while gomez2012 suggested that it could have also generated the vertical bending and breathing waves seen in both pre-Gaia surveys and Gaia widrow2012, williams2013, carlin2013, xu2015, bennett2019 . Several groups have found vertical phase spirals in high-resolution simulations of a Sgr-Milky Way encounter though none of these have managed to reproduce the morphology of the spirals found in the Gaia data laporte2019, bennett2021 .

sources.summary: The paper treats the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as a possible external perturber of the Milky Way phase spiral, noting its disruption and linking the phase-spiral signal to its mass history and impact time.

quotes.quote: The primary observational evidence for this scenario is the presence of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy ibata_dwarf_1994 which is undergoing disruption by the Milky Way binney_origin_2018, laporte_footprints_2019, bland-hawthorn_galah_2019 . If the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy is the cause, then the properties of the phase spiral and the properties of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy at the time when the interaction took place are linked and knowledge of one can be used to derive the properties of the other, for example, the mass history of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy, and the time of impact bland-hawthorn_galactic_2021 .
show the rotation of the phase spiral at different galactic azimuths in their N-body simulation of the effects of the passage of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy on the disc.

The thick line is the Galactic disc and the `` V_Z feature 2'' at L_Z 2700 kpc km s ^ 1 is the bifurcation which was discovered by gaia_collaboration_gaia_2021 and investigated by who found that it may be an effect of the passage of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper excludes Sagittarius dSph because ongoing Milky Way disruption affects its properties, but tests that including Sagittarius off-plane would strengthen the luminosity-gap significance.

quotes.quote: We have instead not included the Sagittarius dSph, as its observed properties are strongly influenced by the ongoing merger with the MW Ibata2001 ; however, we will take it into account when discussing the luminosity distribution of our sample.

A similar argument would also apply if the Sagittarius dSph ( M_V = -13.5 ) were included in the off-plane sample see e.g. for details on its orbital properties Pawlowski+Kroupa2020 . We recall that we have excluded this galaxy from our analysis because its observed properties are strongly influenced by the ongoing tidal disruption caused by the MW.

Even including Sagittarius in the off-plane sample then not only maintains the large gap at the bright-end between the luminosity distributions of the on- and off-plane samples, but in fact does so with high statistical significance (KS and AD tests returning p-values <0.05 ).

sources.summary: The paper compares its satellite-merger simulation with the Sagittarius remnant, stressing it does not reproduce the Milky Way-Sagittarius interaction while noting the Milky Way has experienced multiple Sagittarius pericentric passages.

quotes.quote: In the `late time' snapshot the dwarf galaxy is located at (x,y,z,v_x,v_y,v_z)=(-8.1,1.2,24.8,-117.4,8.6,-30.2) kpc, km s ^ 1 compared to the estimated position and motion of the Sagittarius remnant (x,y,z,v_x,v_y,v_z)=(9.5,-0.4,-6.7,115.7,5.9,311.9) kpc, km s ^ 1 Vasiliev+20 . Thus, the dwarf galaxy in the Simulation is not at the same phase as Sagittarius, and we are not attempting to reproduce the conditions in the Milky Way, merely illustrate the phase spirals following multiple perturbations. In general, we stress that this model is not designed to specifically reproduce the Milky Way Sagittarius interaction for which we refer the reader to the models presented in BBH21,Stelea23 , but rather be a general laboratory for investigating merger dynamics in an otherwise quiet disc.
The Milky Way has experienced multiple pericentric passages from the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy, and contains a central bar and spiral arms.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as a paradigm tidal-disruption case, identified through giant-star velocity clustering and associated with clusters and debris that may extend around the sky.

quotes.quote: One very notable radial velocity-distance clump in the survey of distant stars by iba95 is now recognized as the structural paradigm of the type of tidal events sought here -- an extremely elongated, tidal structure forming through the destruction of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy. Indeed, the Sgr galaxy was first identified through the clustering of radial velocities in distant giant stars , a technique we intend to exploit in the present survey.
One ``family'' of clusters (including Arp 2, Terzan 7 and 8, M54, and Pal 12) is apparently associated with the paradigm tidal event, Sagittarius iba95,dm00 .
As the example of the Sgr galaxy illustrates, the tidal debris of satellite galaxies may stretch to substantial lengths ( mat98,dm00 ), and perhaps completely encircle the sky joh96,joh98,iba00 .

sources.summary: The paper treats the Sgr dwarf as another possible warp driver, comparing its lower mass, closer position, possible initial mass, and orbital-plane orientation with the LMC.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, the Sgr dwarf was posed as another candidate. Though its current mass is much smaller ( 10^9M_ ) than the LMC, its position is closer (20 kpc), thus the gravitational tide is comparable to that of the LMC. jian2000 proposed a possibility that the Sgr dwarf had 10^ 11 M_ initially. Moreover, since the orbital plane of the Sgr dwarf is perpendicular to that of the LMC, the induce warp would have a line of nodes perpendicular to that induced by the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper describes the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal as a Milky Way-merging galaxy with an older Mira population whose PL-based distance modulus agrees with RR Lyrae estimates.

quotes.quote: Leaving the Magellanic Clouds, but staying in the local group we look at C-rich Miras in the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal. This is the galaxy, discovered only in 1994, that is merging with the Milky Way on the far side of the bulge. It contains Miras with periods in the range 230 to 360 days, distinctly shorter than those in Magellanic Cloud clusters, as we might expect from this somewhat older population (Whitelock et al. \ 1999). The distance modulus derived from the Mira PL, (m-M)_0=17.36 0.2 mag, is in good agreement with that from RR Lyrae variables, (m-M)_0=17.18 0.2 mag.

sources.summary: The paper compares Sagittarius RR Lyrae periods and metallicity spread with halo samples, using these properties to test whether a halo clump belongs to Sagittarius debris.

quotes.quote: This means that pulsation properties of RR Lyrae stars in the halo clump can be soundly adopted to single out whether they do belong to the Galactic halo or to Sagittarius. Data plotted in Fig. 1 clearly show that the period distribution of RRc in Sagittarius presents a well-defined peak centered on P 0.32 days. On the other hand, data available in the literature indicate that RRc in the halo present a broad period distribution ranging from 0.2 to 0.4 days and do not show a well defined peak. Oddly enough, the spread in metallicity of RRc stars in the halo is narrower ( Fe/H 1.65 dex with a dispersion of 0.3 dex; skk91 ) than in Sagittarius ( Fe/H 1.0 dex with a dispersion of 0.8 dex; tall02 ).

sources.summary: The paper argues Whiting 1 lies in the Sagittarius dwarf stream and strengthens evidence that Sgr dSph formed six globular clusters over at least 6 Gyr.

quotes.quote: We demonstrate that the position of Whiting 1 on the sky, its distance from the Sun, and its radial velocity are identical to within the errors of both the theoretical predictions of the trailing stream of stars from the Sgr dSph galaxy and the previous observations of the M giant stars that delineate the streams. With the addition of Whiting 1, there is now strong evidence that 6 globular clusters formed within the Sgr dSph. Whiting 1 is particularly interesting because it is the youngest and among the most metal rich.

The relatively young age of Whiting 1 demonstrates that this dwarf galaxy was able to form star clusters for a period of at least 6 Gyr, and the age and metallicity of Whiting 1 are consistent with the age-metallicity relationship in the main body of the Sgr dSph.

sources.summary: The paper uses Sagittarius as a disrupted dwarf-spheroidal example, noting perspective rotation, a tangential-velocity estimate, distorted contours, unbound stars, and a constant velocity dispersion along its major axis.

quotes.quote: In our chosen Galactic potential, all these orbits lie within the core radius d=12 kpc of the halo component (see equation [ halo ] ), so its tidal field is roughly constant along the orbit and halo shocking is unimportant (in contrast, this is likely to be an important factor in the evolution of the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy -- see Johnston, Spergel Hernquist 1995).

In models, run along eccentric orbits, of the disruption of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy, Johnston, Spergel Hernquist (1995) found that the line of sight velocity gradient is dominated by perspective rotation, despite the fact that our viewpoint is farther from the satellite than the centre of the Galaxy, presumably because the satellite is not co-rotating. Vel azquez White (1995) made an estimate of 225km s ^ 1 for the tangential velocity of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy by considering only the effect of perspective rotation and this roughly agrees with the observed proper motion of 250km s ^ 1 .

The Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy is an example of a system where it can be plausibly argued that this behaviour is observed: its highly distorted surface density contours suggest that it is likely to be surrounded by a cloud of unbound stars and its velocity dispersion is roughly constant along the entire length of its major axis .

sources.summary: The paper describes Sagittarius as a dwarf galaxy currently being disrupted by the Milky Way, with about ten associated globular clusters and M 54 generally identified as its nucleus.

quotes.quote: In particular, we are currently observing the disruption of dwarf galaxy Sagittarius by tidal forces from the Galaxy. About ten globular clusters are associated with this galaxy. The massive globular cluster M 54 is generally believed to be the nucleus of the system. The galactic orbital elements of same else clusters also suggest that they were captured from various satellite galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper cites the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as a nearby dSph whose core structure, pericentric orbit, and possible pre-existing bar are consistent with tidal-stirring interpretations.

quotes.quote: Recently, the structure and kinematics of the stellar core of the nearby Sagittarius dwarf galaxy have also been successfully modeled within the framework of tidal stirring Lokas_etal10a .
Interestingly, the pericentric distance of the tightest orbit with r_ peri = 17 kpc roughly corresponds to that inferred for the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy e.g., Law_etal05 .
Recently, the strongly non-spherical shape of the core of the disrupting nearby Sagittarius dSph has also been ascribed to a pre-existing bar Lokas_etal10a .

sources.summary: The paper uses the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as an observational constraint on chameleon-gravity equivalence-principle violations, citing its tidal-stream symmetry, expected circular-velocity difference, and current sensitivity limits.

quotes.quote: Based on the symmetry of the tidal streams of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy, argue that the difference between these accelerations should be < 10 , which implies <5 difference in circular velocities. Given that v_ vir 15 km/s, and M 10^9 M_ for Sagittarius dwarf , we expect around 3 difference in circular velocities (see Fig. vel ), which is just below the observational limit, assuming that Sagittarius orbit is just outside a chameleon thin shell.

While current bounds, based on the observations of the tidal streams of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy, are not yet sensitive to this effect, future surveys of kinematics in the Milky Way halo can dramatically strengthen these limits .

sources.summary: The review describes Sagittarius as a dwarf galaxy merging with the Milky Way whose stars show copper deficiency and declining alpha abundances at high metallicity.

quotes.quote: The same is the case for the more metal-rich part of stars in the globular cluster Centauri (Cunha et al. 2002), and stars belonging to the Sagittarius dSph galaxy (Sbordone et al. 2007) and the Large Magellanic Cloud (Pomp e ia et al. 2008) as shown in Fig. fig:Cu-Fe .

The other example is the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy, which was discovered by Ibata et al. (1994) to be merging with the Milky Way. The abundances derived by Sbordone et al. (2007) for giant stars show a declining trend of [ /Fe ] reaching [ /Fe ] as low as 0.3 dex at solar metallicity.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Sagittarius as an inner satellite with HST proper motion, an orbit nearly perpendicular to the other satellites, and poor alignment with VPOS-3.

quotes.quote: Compared to the PM list compiled by Metz2008 , we also include a new HST based PM measurement for Sagittarius 2010AJ....139..839P and an additional ground-based PM measurement for Fornax 2011AJ....142...93M .
As has also already been discussed by Metz2008 , Sagittarius orbits approximately perpendicularly to the majority of the MW satellites.
Of the 11 satellites, only Sagittarius cannot be closely aligned with the VPOS-3 plane; the other satellites can have orbital planes inclined by only 20^ \ or less from the VPOS-3.

sources.summary: The review identifies Sgr dSph as a well-constrained Local Group harassment case, with tidal arms throughout the Galactic halo and a defined age-metallicity relation.

quotes.quote: The other obvious case is the one of the Sgr dSph (Ibata et al. 1994, Majewski, this conference), whose orbit is better constrained. The tidal arms are traced all over the Galactic halo (Majewski et al. 2004) , and the Age-Metallicty relation (AMR, a consequence of the SFH) well defined. These examples, together with the Magellanic system and the Srg dSph, illustrate convincingly that DG in the LG are undergoing profound transformations.

sources.summary: The paper notes Sagittarius dSph as the only dwarf spheroidal with a known tidal stream, near pericentre, requiring N-body simulations beyond the equilibrium tidal-track method.

quotes.quote: To date the only dwarf spheroidal with a known tidal stream corresponds to the Sagittarius dSph Ibata1997 , see Belokurov2014 for a recent data compilation. As this dSph is currently located close to pericentre, we can't apply directly our tidal evolutionary tracks, as these have been computed for dSphs that have reached equilibrium after each pericentre passage. The use of N body simulations will be required to study perturbations from the equilibrium configuration.

sources.summary: The paper considers whether Be 29 and Sau 1 are associated with the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy, noting compatible positions and kinematics but abundance mismatches that make Sgr dSph an unlikely origin.

quotes.quote: A study by Carraro09 investigated the possibility that Be 29 and Sau 1 are associated with the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy or Sgr dSph, Ibata94 , which is currently being accreted on the Galactic disk, rather than with Monoceros. They showed that their position and kinematics are compatible with an origin within Sgr dSph, and their [ Fe/H ] and [ /Fe ] abundances are marginally compatible with being in the metal-rich tail of the Sgr dSph population, although the Ca abundances of those objects do not match the subsolar [ Ca/Fe ] reported by Monaco07 and Sbordone07 for Sgr dSph. The two known dwarf galaxies with metallicities above -1 (Sgr dSph and Fornax) in fact exhibit subsolar abundances of about 0.2 dex see e.g. and references therein Tolstoy09 , making them unlikely candidates for the origin of our outer-disk open clusters.

sources.summary: The paper describes the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as a Milky Way satellite discovered on the far side of the Milky Way, with tidal features responsible for a prominent Galactic-halo stellar stream.

quotes.quote: The Sagittarius dwarf galaxy was discovered relatively recently due to its position on the far side of the Milky Way MW; Ibata1994 . Its extended morphology towards the MW plane suggested the existence of extra tidal features Johnston1995,Lynden-Bell1995,Mateo1996 . The Two Micron All-Sky Survey (2MASS) and the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) made it clear that this dwarf is responsible for the most conspicuous tidal stellar substructure present in the Galactic halo Newberg2002,Majewski2003 .

sources.summary: The paper treats the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as a possible analogue of its high-density model C, noting its older stellar population and suggesting high initial density and star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: The Sagittarius dwarf galaxy is a candidate for galaxies like model C because it has process elements only in [ Fe/H ] > -1 if we can sample enough number of stars which have been a member of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy since it was formed. Most stars in the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy are older than 5 Gyr 2015MNRAS.451.3489D . The Sagittarius dwarf galaxy might have formed with a high initial density and formed stars with a high SFR.

sources.summary: The paper describes the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy with its estimated current mass and pericentre, possible prior mass loss on a second passage, and an Aquarius-D2 analogue at 20 kpc.

quotes.quote: The Milky Way's Sagittarius dwarf galaxy (Sgr) has an estimated current total mass M_ Sgr 5 10^8 M_ and a pericentre of 20 kpc law . If we assume Sgr to be on its second passage, then it has probably lost 50 of its mass during the initial crossing. In the Aq-D2, an analogue to Sgr can be found at 20 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper describes Sagittarius dSph as a Local Group ultra-diffuse-galaxy candidate, giving its distance, magnitude, effective radius, and central surface brightness.

quotes.quote: In the literature, two UDG candidates are found in the census of faint galaxies in the Local Group: Sagittarius dSph McConnachie12 and Andromeda 10 10 10 2 Martin13 . Sagittarius dSph is 26 kpc away from us, with V band absolute magnitude of M_V=-13.5 mag, r_ e =2.6 kpc, and V band _0 25.2 \ mag/arcsec^2 . This UDG candidate has been reported by , who are the first authors to identify UDGs in the Local Group.

sources.summary: The paper links Pal 12 and its surrounding overdensity to the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal, whose disruption produces tidal tails and preferentially strips old, metal-poor populations.

quotes.quote: Conversely, Irwin99 pointed out that distance and radial velocity of Pal 12 are consistent with the hypothesis that it has been captured by our Galaxy in a tidal interaction with the Sagittarius dwarf Spheroidal (Sgr dSph) galaxy.

As stated above, it has been proved with many different tracers that the disruption of the Sgr dSph galaxy produces huge tidal tails extending for many tens of kiloparsecs from the parent galaxy, with tidally stripped stars wrapping a full 360 ^ around the celestial sphere see, e.g.

This hypothesis is in agreement with Bellazzini06b who, analysing the horizontal branch stars in the core of the Sgr galaxy and in a wide field located tens of kpc away from the center of the galaxy itself, concluded that old and metal-poor populations appear to be preferentially stripped from the Sgr galaxy during the past peri-Galactic passages with respect to the intermediate-age intermediate-metallicity population that presently dominates its bound core.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy as the Sgr stream progenitor, describing its disruption history, extended dark halo, remaining bound mass, and northern tidal debris.

quotes.quote: The first modeling attempt was done by , however, finding the progenitor, the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy, disrupting after only two orbits while observations show the completion of about 10 orbits. As a solution to the problem, concluded from an extensive numerical study that the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy must have a stiff and extended dark matter halo if it has still about 25 of its initial mass and is still bound today. Early pencil-beam surveys before the large-scale survey era were used by , and , reporting detections of tidal debris in the northern stream of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy and leading to the publication of one of the first models of the Sagittarius stream being in good agreement with the observations Martinez2004 .

sources.summary: The paper links the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy to strong Milky Way disc flaring, describing its orbit, passages, and modeled interactions as drivers of vertical and radial disc motions.

quotes.quote: While some small level of flaring is expected for extended discs built up by radial migration, we demonstrate that the very strong flaring of the Milky Way disc that we observe is likely more consistent with it being dynamically heated by the repeated passage of the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy through the midplane.
We suggest that this very strong flaring may be consistent with the dynamical heating of the disc caused by the orbit of the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy (Sgr dSph) around the Galaxy.

In these models, the Sgr dSph produces vertical oscillations as well as coupled radial motions in the disc donghia_2016 through the response of the MW halo weinberg_1998, vesperini_2000, gomez_2016 to its infall and eventually through its tidal interactions.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the Sagittarius dwarf as the probable perturbation source for Milky Way phase spirals, citing its trefoil orbit, recent disc crossings, and value for constraining its orbit and mass.

quotes.quote: As discussed in the previous works, the most probably perturbation source is the Sagittarius dwarf, which has a "trefoil" orbit over the past 2.3 Gyr. It crossed the disc about 420 Myr ago at R = 13 kpc and transited again about 50 Myr ago joss, Law2005,Tepper2018 . If the Sagittarius dwarf is responsible for the phase spirals, the results presented here can help constrain its orbit and mass, even the shape of the Galactic halo.

sources.summary: The paper argues that Sag I has an unreliable simulated infall time, is being tidally disrupted, and would have rapid post-infall quenching if a later infall time is adopted.

quotes.quote: Closer inspection of Sag I suggests that its infall time (and thus quenching timescale) is not reliably measured via comparison to subhalos in the pELVIS simulation suite.

In the case of a system such as Sag I that is being disrupted via tidal interaction with the MW, the corresponding subhalo population in the pELVIS simulations is quickly stripped, whereas Sag I is better able to resist disruption due its non-negligible baryonic mass (Lazar et al., in prep).

If we assume t_ infall 5 Gyr for Sag I, instead of our estimate of t_ infall 11 Gyr , we find a quenching timescale of _ quenching 1.6 Gyr , consistent with rapid suppression of star formation following infall.

sources.summary: The review cites the Sgr dwarf galaxy as a nearby resolved NSC case, with broad stellar ages and metallicities, and as a stripped satellite whose NSC M54 was detected first.

quotes.quote: This is most apparent in the two nearest NSCs at the center of our Milky Way and the Sgr dwarf galaxy, where stars can be resolved individually. In both cases, their stars span a wide range of ages and metallicities e.g. and Alfaro-Cuello in this proceedings siegel07,pfuhl11,feldmeier-krause15 .
The Sgr dwarf galaxy is an example of a snapshot in this process ibata94 , with its NSC (the globular cluster M54) being the first part of the galaxy to be detected.

sources.summary: The Sagittarius dwarf is presented as a tidally stirred Milky Way satellite whose structure and kinematics are reproduced after a second pericenter passage, leaving a bar-like, weakly rotating stellar component.

quotes.quote: Obvious candidates for tidally stirred dwarfs in the vicinity of the Milky Way include the Sagittarius and Carina dwarfs, although most of dSph galaxies in the Local Group show signs of tidal extensions. In the case of the Sagittarius dwarf it has been demonstrated that its present structural and kinematic properties can be reproduced by a model in which the initially disky progenitor has just passed the second pericenter on its orbit around the Milky Way ( ). In this model the stellar component still preserves its bar-like shape (slightly inclined with respect to the orbit) and shows very little rotation.

sources.summary: The paper uses Sagittarius as the benchmark accreted dwarf galaxy: its stars and associated globular clusters have low Sc, V, and Zn abundances that the proposed chemical tagging identifies.

quotes.quote: The iron-peak elements Sc, V, and in particular Zn were proposed as an alternative to elements to tag accreted GCs in the metal-rich regime, where the dwarf galaxy Sagittarius and its GCs show peculiarly marked under-abundances of these heavier species with respect to Milky Way stars.
Using a large set of GC abundances from the literature, we also show that the new method with Sc, V, and Zn seems to be efficient in picking up GCs related to the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy.

Minelli et al. (2021a: M21a) recently proposed using the iron-peak elements Sc, V, and Zn to explore the metal-rich ( [ Fe/H ] 1 dex) regime since they observed large differences in the abundances between stars in the MW and those in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), as well as in the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy (Sag) in this metallicity range.

sources.summary: The paper cites Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal pericentre passages and a Milky Way-Sagittarius dynamical interaction as possible triggers for recent Galactic disc star formation and gas infall.

quotes.quote: The authors proposed that the timing of these enhanced SF episodes is consistent with the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy pericentre passages which triggered the SF in the Galactic disc e.g., laporte2019b,antoja2020 .

The authors proposed that the timing of these peaks of star formation coincides with the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy pericentre passages which triggered formation of new stars in the Galactic disc.
